verdict_one_line: Proceed — but the strongest honest case rests on one under-argued mech-interp fact: the workspace's ~25-dim, ≤10%-variance geometry is precisely the enabling condition that makes Phase 4's coverage objective non-degenerate, while the weak form's fp16-offline + text-injection design makes the loudly-flagged quantization risk a Phase-5-only problem.

points.title: The ~25-vector / ≤10%-variance workspace is what makes Phase 4 coverage NON-degenerate
points.claim: The paper's median-~25-active-vectors, ≤10%-of-activation-variance 'Workspace Band' is not just a caution (as the triad table frames it) — it is the affirmative reason to believe Phase 4's facility-location/coverage objective beats relevance top-k. In high-dim BGE-M3 space (1024-d) points are near-orthogonal, so max-coverage collapses to max-sum-relevance and the submodular objective buys nothing; coverage vs relevance only DIVERGE when the target region is low-dimensional and points genuinely overlap in their span. A ~25-dim bounded workspace is exactly that regime. So the review's own 'coverage in J-directions, not PCA directions' note has a sharper implication it doesn't state: the low workspace dimensionality is a prerequisite for Phase 4 to pay at all, and the paper's 'same J-lens vector serves many downstream computations' (broadcast) is literally the set-cover structure Phase 4 assumes.
points.evidence: Review subspace-triad row 2 (~25 J-lens vectors, ≤10% variance, broadcast); Phase 4 roadmap (submodular greedy coverage). Geometric near-orthogonality of high-dim vectors is standard. Repo: assembler is per-document relevance+budget today (context_manager.py:1701-1712).
points.targets: Phase 4 (bet A payoff); decision point on 'coverage over Jacobian directions'

points.title: Quantization drift (Open Q#1) is central to Phase 5 ONLY, not the weak form
points.claim: The review elevates GGUF/quantization drift to 'the central risk' for bet A wholesale. That over-weights it. Phases 0-4 never touch the quantized model's internals: Phase 0 calibrates on fp16/bf16 under HF transformers, Phase 2 exists precisely to buy query-time no-internals, and Phases 3-4 fit splats offline and inject TEXT. What matters for the weak form is not 'does the Q4 model's workspace equal its fp16 parent's' but 'does text selected to cover the fp16 workspace still make the Q4 model answer better' — an end-to-end question, and the WORST case is a clean null (the jspace tier tunes to ~0 weight under RRF, which is Phase 3's own declared fallback). The same context-robustness the roadmap lists as Phase 4's downside risk ('decoders robust to context composition') is what PROTECTS Phases 0-4 from quantization drift. Drift is only catastrophic in Phase 5, where a soft prefix computed against fp16 is garbage for Q4 — and Phase 5 is already stack-gated (llama.cpp/Ollama can't inject anyway) and legibility-capped. Net: the risk register should move quantization drift from 'primary bet-A risk' to 'primary Phase-5 risk.'
points.evidence: Roadmap Phase 0 method ('NOT Ollama... requires fp16/bf16'); Phase 2 goal (no query-time internals); Phase 3 risk/fallback ('RRF drives weight to ~0 = clean negative'); Phase 4 risk ('decoders robust to context composition'); Phase 5 constraints (Ollama/llama.cpp = no). Review §'New risks' #elevate-Open-Q#1.
points.targets: Open Q#1; Phase 5 vs Phases 0-4; council decision points #3 and #5

points.title: Linear probe BGE-M3→J-lens is the cheapest path to bet A on the default stack, and mech-interp predicts it partly works
points.claim: Phase 2 fallback (b) — 'if a linear probe from BGE-M3 works, J-space becomes a learned re-metric, a d×r matrix, no new encoder' — is treated by both roadmap and review as merely a 'philosophically deflationary' consolation. It is actually the highest-EV Phase 2 experiment and should run FIRST. Mechanistic reason: the J-lens is E[∂h_final/∂h_ℓ] — the Jacobian of the state the unembedding then reads out, i.e. 'what the model is poised to verbalize.' Composed with the unembedding it is vocabulary/semantics-aligned BY CONSTRUCTION — the same next-token-semantic geometry a retrieval encoder like BGE-M3 is trained to approximate. So nonzero linear recoverability from BGE-M3 is the mechanistically-EXPECTED outcome, not a lucky one. If it lands, the entire query-time internals problem on the default Ollama stack collapses to a stored d×r matrix — and the schema ALREADY has the slot: spaces(... basis BLOB ...). The upside is real and the plumbing is prepaid.
points.evidence: Roadmap Phase 2 fallback (b) + Open Q#2; review triad row 2 (J-lens = coordinate-corrected Jacobian of output logit). Schema slot verified: roadmap Phase 1 spaces table 'basis BLOB'. J-lens→unembedding→logit composition per Anthropic paper (verified read, review §'What the paper establishes').
points.targets: Phase 2 (fallback b → promote to first experiment); council decision point #2

points.title: Bet B / Phase 1 is verified greenfield-but-clean; the density-feature extension is a 4th application of a proven slot
points.claim: 'Lead with bet B' is even cheaper than the review argues, and I can ground it in the repo. (1) There is NO Mahalanobis / whitening / Ledoit-Wolf / covariance code anywhere in helix_context — bet B is net-new but conflict-free. (2) splats and spaces tables are absent from the DDL, and adding them follows the EXISTING side-table pattern (genome_calibration, harmonic_links are precedent scoring/calibration side-tables, not columns on genes — exactly what the roadmap specifies). (3) The 'ship density as a 6th logistic feature before any pure density gate' step is the FOURTH time this exact extension is applied: DEFAULT_BETAS in know_calibration.py already grew from a 4-feature to 5-feature to 6-tuple logistic (Stage 6→7 added freshness as beta5, the code literally carries STAGE-7-EXT markers). Adding density as the next beta + recalibrating via the existing scripts/calibrate_know_confidence.py is a worn groove, not new risk. This is the concrete engineering evidence behind recommendation #1.
points.evidence: Grep: no mahalanobis|whiten|ledoit|covariance in helix_context. ddl.py has genes:59, genome_calibration:712, harmonic_links:689 — no splats/spaces. know_calibration.py:64 DEFAULT_BETAS is a 6-tuple; docstring §'STAGE-7-EXT' documents the 4→5→6 growth; scripts/calibrate_know_confidence.py exists (referenced CLAUDE.md).
points.targets: Phase 1 (know path); council recommendation #1 (lead with bet B)

points.title: Phase 4's mechanism is ~80% prepaid in embedding space — but the cheap dry-run tests machinery, not the hypothesis
points.claim: Phase 4 does not start from scratch. The variable-per-document budget the coverage objective needs already ships: _compute_foveated_caps (context_manager.py:341) computes rank-proportional per-document char caps and the splice loop already honors them (target = foveated_caps[idx]*foveated_base, else 1000). The per-fragment keep/drop hook the roadmap says Phase 4 should 'mirror' — interference_trim (cymatics.py:526) — already exists AND already has the complement-fallback the answer-loss bug needs (result[gene_id] = doc.complement or doc.content[:500]). So a coverage-greedy selector can be prototyped by swapping only the cap/keep DECISION, and it can be measured end-to-end in BGE-M3 space BEFORE any J-space exists — cheaper than the roadmap's 0→1→2→3→4 ordering. HONEST CONCESSION (ties to point 1): coverage in 1024-d is near-degenerate, so an embedding-space dry-run validates the plumbing but CANNOT falsify J-coverage — a null there is uninformative about the bet. Use it to de-risk engineering, not the hypothesis.
points.evidence: Verified: context_manager.py:341 _compute_foveated_caps, :1701-1706 foveated vs 1000-char path; cymatics.py:526 interference_trim with complement fallback at :556/:579/:584; headroom_bridge.py:227 content[:target_chars] fallback (the query-agnostic cut). Fuser.add_tier zero-weight no-op (fusion.py:104-108) makes jspace tier insertion clean.
points.targets: Phase 4 (engineering ordering); Phase 3 (fusion.py add_tier integration)

points.title: The eval-harness CODE is real, but the review's grounding NUMBERS are uncommitted — pre-register against committed data
points.claim: The review calls the eval harness 'half-built' and it is right that the code skeleton exists and is load-bearing: bench_needle.py carries the NEEDLES list + check_gold_delivery/find_needle scorer, and s2_sike_bedsweep_run.py drives it server-per-config. That genuinely front-loads Phase 0's 'single highest-leverage engineering item.' BUT the two documents the review leans on for its quantitative claims — docs/benchmarks/2026-07-06-sike-run2-fts-depth-fusion.md (source of 'RRF 0.72 vs additive 0.58') and 2026-07-06-faithfulness-experiment-runbook.md (the 'cheap version of Open Q#3') — are NOT in the repo (latest benchmark doc is 2026-07-05). Before Phase 0 pre-registers its ≥5-point success bar 'vs the full RRF stack,' commit those runbooks so the baseline is a committed artifact, not a quoted-but-unlanded number. This also directly grounds the vocabulary-ceiling caution: the needles ARE multi-token identifiers (accept-lists 'PostgreSQL 16', '51/52'), so bet A's vocab-bounded J-lens is structurally weakest exactly on Helix's current gaps — scope bet A to conceptual retrieval and leave identifier lookup to FTS5.
points.evidence: Verified present: benchmarks/bench_needle.py NEEDLES:54, PostgreSQL-16 accept:306-308, 51/52 expected/accept:516-517; scripts/bench_chain/s2_sike_bedsweep_run.py imports bench_needle. Verified ABSENT: glob docs/benchmarks/*.md returns no 2026-07-06 file (newest is 2026-07-05-sike-bedsweep-issue-resolutions.md); no faithfulness or sike-run2 file anywhere.
points.targets: Phase 0 (pre-registration integrity); bet A scoping (vocabulary ceiling)

specialty: Mechanistic interpretability (J-space / Jacobian-lens, activation geometry, quantization transfer)
verdict_one_line: Bet A is mis-scoped at its only real test — a J-lens is a coordinate-corrected backward-pass Jacobian, not the forward-pass activation capture Phase 0 budgets — and it aims an output-verbalization basis at Helix's input-side identifier-retrieval gap; lead with and cap at bet B's Mahalanobis whitening, which needs neither J-space nor the decoder.

points.title: Phase 0's Jacobian probe is a backward-pass object mis-budgeted as a forward pass — the only probe that tests the output-shaping hypothesis is off by an order of magnitude
points.claim: The review's own triad defines the workspace as the coordinate-corrected Jacobian E[dh_final/dh_l], but Phase 0's Method step 1 captures 'mean-pooled residual activations' via output_hidden_states — a forward pass. Those are different computations: the J-lens needs per-token vector-Jacobian products (backward passes) with the coordinate-correction that subtracts the frozen-attention path, not activations. The review patched Phase 0 to add 'a small actual Jacobian J-lens computation on one fp16 model' but left the S-M effort tag and the output_hidden_states method intact. There is no decoder-under-HF infrastructure to build this on: torch/transformers are optional encoder-only extras used solely by the BGE-M3/SPLADE/DeBERTa/NLI backends. So the activation-PCA half (which the review says is >=90% disjoint from the workspace) is cheap and buildable, and the Jacobian half — the ONLY half that bears on Bet A's output-shaping thesis — is an unbudgeted mechanistic-interp research project.
points.evidence: roadmap Phase 0 Method step 1 (mean-pooled activations, output_hidden_states, lines 72-76); review triad row 21 (J-space = coordinate-corrected Jacobian of logit w.r.t. hidden state) and line 24 (PCA finds the activation-variance subspace, >=90% disjoint from J-space); pyproject.toml:54,96-97 (torch/transformers are nli/full extras); helix_context uses them only in backends/{bgem3_codec,splade_backend,deberta_backend,nli_backend}.py — no decoder hidden-state path exists
points.targets: Phase 0 (kill-shot) + Decision point #1 (accept subspace split) / #5 (which fp16 model)

points.title: The ~25-vector workspace is a contextual, per-token managed quantity — a static per-gene J-coordinate captured offline measures the wrong conditional distribution
points.claim: The paper's 'median ~25 active vectors' and the whole workspace story are properties of a forward pass on a specific prompt: the paper shows directed modulation and that 'information can be pulled in when the task requires it.' The roadmap's premise (Phase 2: 'genes are static; batch a forward pass over the corpus') computes each gene's J-coordinates in isolation, with no query and no task. A gene's workspace occupancy when embedded alone is not the same as its occupancy as one of 7000 injected tokens answering a specific query — the workspace is actively managed by context. So the offline per-gene J-coordinate is not a stable function of the gene text; it is a function of gene-plus-context. This undercuts the calibration premise that makes Phase 2's query-projection head necessary in the first place.
points.evidence: review lines 28-29 (median ~25 active vectors, directed modulation, 'information can be pulled in when the task requires it', model actively manages the workspace); roadmap Phase 2 goal lines 142-146 ('genes are static; batch a forward pass over the corpus'); contrast with know_decision.py coordinate_confidence being context-derived
points.targets: Phase 2 (query-side J-projection, tau>=0.8 bar) + Phase 3 offline gene-splat fit

points.title: Phase 4 optimizes input-context coverage over an output-verbalization basis, and defers testing that this is even coherent until after the machinery is built
points.claim: The J-lens is 'what the model is poised to verbalize' — a next-token, vocabulary-bounded output object. Helix retrieves documents to inject as INPUT context, not to predict as output (context_manager splice/assemble). Selecting input fragments to 'cover the J-directions the query needs to broadcast' presumes the input->residual map is invertible/controllable enough that input-side coverage steers output-side workspace occupancy — which is exactly Open Q#3. Phase 4 builds the greedy facility-location selector first and relegates that foundational check to a SECONDARY measurement read out afterward. The roadmap's own risk line concedes the likely outcome ('decoders are robust to context composition; shaping doesn't matter once the right facts are present'), and the vocabulary ceiling means J-targeting is weakest precisely on the multi-token identifier needles (claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, PostgreSQL 16) that are Helix's actual measured gap.
points.evidence: roadmap Phase 4 lines 211-224 (coverage objective) and 238-242 (secondary measurement is a post-build readout); roadmap Phase 4 Risk lines 244-248; review risk #2 (J-lens vocabulary ceiling, lines 47,64) and Open Q#3 (lines 319-324); Helix's needles are multi-token identifiers per review line 47
points.targets: Phase 4 (coverage-shaped assembly) + Open Q#3 (text->residual controllability)

points.title: Quantization drift is not generic drift — the <=10%-variance workspace is where Q4 rounding error lands proportionally hardest, and Helix's default stack is GGUF
points.claim: The review reports the workspace is <=10% of activation variance — a low-variance causal bottleneck — while the high-variance directions are task-agnostic (the anisotropy that motivates Mahalanobis). Quantization noise (Q4_K_M) is roughly uniform per-weight; a low-variance subspace has the worst signal-to-quantization-noise ratio, so the workspace is mechanistically the MOST fragile part of the representation under GGUF quantization. Helix's default upstream is Ollama on :11434 (GGUF), and the bench drives an 'Ollama rung.' Calibrating J-space on an fp16 parent and serving Q4 is therefore not a mild offset — there is a prior that drift concentrates in the exact subspace being calibrated. The roadmap treats the fp16-vs-Q4 comparison as a 'bonus probe at small cost' inside Phase 0; the review elevates it to primary but phase ordering does not enforce that as a gate.
points.evidence: review line 28 (J-space <=10% of activation variance, limited-capacity bottleneck) and line 35 (high-variance directions are task-agnostic); roadmap Open Q#1 lines 303-309 ('bonus probe... at small cost'); review line 79 (elevate quantization drift to primary); config.py:187 upstream=http://localhost:11434 (Ollama/GGUF); s2_sike_bedsweep_run.py:203 'Ollama rung'
points.targets: Open Q#1 (quantization stability) + Phase 3 profile-vs-per-artifact management + Decision point #5

points.title: Bet B's density gate re-derives, in a weaker geometry-only form, signals the know-logistic and freshness gate already carry — its independent lift is likely near-collinear
points.claim: The know path is already a 5-feature logistic whose features include coordinate_confidence (folder+file-grain match) and freshness_min (Stage-7 decay across the retrieved top-K), and a freshness gate module already demotes stale/superseded docs. Mahalanobis log-density measures typicality on the BGE-M3 semantic manifold, which is trained for similarity, not correctness or recency: a semantically-typical stale-or-wrong document scores low-anomaly = high-density = passes the gate. The review flags the adversarial-poisoning version of this; the sharper point is the NORMAL stale-doc case the freshness gate exists to catch. So density likely enters as a near-collinear 6th feature with small independent beta, not a dominating signal. The mechanistically-honest scope is exactly the roadmap's own fallback — density as a 6th logistic feature, measure delta-ECE — and the pure-density-gate ambition should be dropped up front rather than after a head-to-head.
points.evidence: know_calibration.py:265-278 (5-feature logistic: top_score, score_gap, lexical_dense_agree, coordinate_confidence, freshness_min); know_decision.py:318-347 (feature signatures); retrieval/freshness.py exists (Stage 7 demotion); review risk #1 lines 63 (density gate blind to poisoning); roadmap Phase 1 fallback lines 118-122 (ship density as 6th logistic feature first)
points.targets: Phase 1 (density know/go, 'density dominates risk-coverage' bar) + Decision point #2 (lead with bet B)

points.title: A distributional per-gene representation with a per-fragment scoring hook already exists (cymatics) — Phase 1/4 risk building a second, parallel one without an integration story
points.claim: Phase 1 frames genes as currently point-estimates to be replaced by a 'soft distribution (mean + covariance).' But cymatics already superposes each gene's terms into a 256-bin Gaussian spectrum (a distributional, non-point representation) with cosine/W1 scoring, and already owns interference_trim — the exact per-fragment hook Phase 4 says it will 'mirror.' Meanwhile the on-ingest gene embedding is a 20-d SEMA vector and the dense path is BGE-M3 1024-d; a splat mu+Sigma fit in either space is still not a decoder residual stream. The roadmap never reconciles the new splat distribution with the existing cymatics spectrum, so the realistic outcome is two parallel distributional gene representations competing in the fuser — added surface area and maintenance, not a clean replacement. This is the 'wasted effort' failure mode independent of whether J-space works.
points.evidence: cymatics.py:104-167 (build_spectrum: 256-bin Gaussian superposition per gene) and cymatics.py:526 interference_trim; roadmap Phase 4 line 227 names interference_trim as the hook to mirror; roadmap Phase 1 lines 96-100 ('replace the point-estimate gene'); sema_codec.py:1 (20-d SEMA vector); knowledge_store.py:471-473 (BGE-M3 1024-d)
points.targets: Phase 1 (splat genes) + Phase 4 (coverage selector reusing the cymatics fragment hook)

specialty: Mechanistic interpretability (J-space validity, Jacobian-lens vs activation-PCA, workspace-band occupancy, vocabulary ceiling, quantization transfer)
verdict_one_line: Bet B is mechanistically sound and code-ready; bet A's J-space half is measuring the wrong quantity with the wrong instrument at three separate phases, and its payoff test is validated on exactly the content the mechanism cannot represent.

points.title: Phase 0's stated instrument (output_hidden_states) cannot compute the workspace at all
points.claim: The review flags Phase 0 as 'PCA finds activation-variance, not the J-lens' but understates it: the roadmap's Method (line 69-76) specifies capture 'under HF transformers with output_hidden_states', which yields FORWARD activations only. The J-lens is E[∂h_final/∂h_ℓ] — a Jacobian expectation requiring BACKWARD VJPs to the final layer per token (functorch/autograd), a categorically different and much costlier capture. So the roadmap's own proposed instrumentation is forward-only; you literally cannot get the workspace, even as the review's suggested 'small Jacobian bonus probe', without rewriting the capture. Any Phase 0 that keeps output_hidden_states is testing the activation-variance re-metric (bet-B-adjacent), never bet A.
points.evidence: roadmap lines 69-82 (Method: output_hidden_states, then SVD/PCA); review triad line 21-24 (J-space 'not found by activation PCA'); Anthropic J-lens = E[∂h_final/∂h_ℓ]
points.targets: Phase 0 (bet A); decision-point #1 (re-scope Phase 0) and #2 (verify who computes the Jacobian)

points.title: The workspace is a query-conditional decoding transient — breaks Phase 3's 'genes are static, batch offline' premise
points.claim: Phase 3 (lines 283-289) and Open Q#2 assume gene J-coordinates are precomputable offline because 'genes are static; batch a forward pass over the corpus'. But the paper's own findings — directed modulation PULLS concepts into the workspace task-dependently, occupancy plateaus at ~25 mid-band and COLLAPSES at output — mean a gene has no stable, query-independent J-footprint; its active vectors depend on the decoding context it is read under. This also makes Phase 0's mean-pooling risk worse than the roadmap admits (line 89-92): the review's fix (windowed pooling over a CAZ) addresses DEPTH (layers) but not POSITION (tokens) — mean-pooling a gene still averages over token positions that each carry a DIFFERENT sparse 25-vector code. A precomputed offline gene splat in J-space is fitting a distribution to a quantity that only exists at query time.
points.evidence: roadmap lines 142-143, 283-289 (offline gene J-coords), 89-92 (mean-pooling risk); review line 28-29 (directed modulation, pull-in, ~25 median occupancy, collapse at output), line 36 (CAZ depth-extension)
points.targets: Phase 3 (offline splat fit in J-space) and Phase 0 pooling; Open Q#2

points.title: '~25 vectors', '~16 directions', and covariance-rank-k are three different numbers — Phase 3's LOD validation is a category error
points.claim: Even after the review's weight/activation/J-space triad, a fourth conflation survives INSIDE the J-space column. '~25 active vectors' is median OCCUPANCY per token — a sparse code over a large overcomplete J-lens dictionary — NOT the dimensionality of J-space. UWSH's ~16-32 is a WEIGHT-spectral rank. Phase 1's splat k≈8-16 is a per-gene chunk-SCATTER covariance rank. Phase 3 (lines 199-203) proposes: 'if quality at r=4 ≈ r=16, the 16-direction budget story is confirmed in-store' — but r there is the covariance truncation rank; confirming a gene's chunk scatter is low-rank says nothing about workspace occupancy or weight rank. The LOD device-budget mapping (mean/r=4/r=16, line 194) is picking a covariance rank with no principled tie to either the 25 or the 16.
points.evidence: roadmap lines 194 (LOD r→device), 199-203 (r=4≈r=16 'confirms 16-direction'), 114-123 (splat k≈8-16); review triad lines 20-22 (~16-32 weight vs ~25 J-lens), line 28 (median ~25 ACTIVE)
points.targets: Phase 3 LOD validation; Phase 1 splat rank; decision-point #1

points.title: The vocabulary ceiling is adversarially aligned with the Phase 4 validation set — bet A is validated on its worst case
points.claim: The J-lens is the Jacobian of a SINGLE next-token logit w.r.t. hidden state — inherently a single-vocab-token projection. Multi-BPE identifiers (SIKE needles like claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, PostgreSQL 16) have no single J-lens direction; they are emitted autoregressively, each token's J-vector conditioned on the prior. So Phase 4's secondary measurement and Open Q#3 ('does the answer concept enter J-space?', lines 240-242, 320-324) are not well-posed for identifier needles. Phase 4's pre-registered payoff bar (≥5% relative EM, line 238) therefore tests bet A end-to-end on precisely the answer class the mechanism cannot represent. This is stronger than the review's 'weakest on identifiers' (line 47): the chosen eval is adversarial to the mechanism, so a Phase 4 null result would be uninformative about bet A's real value on conceptual retrieval.
points.evidence: roadmap lines 236-242 (Phase 4 EM bar + J-space secondary measurement), 320-324 (Open Q#3); review lines 47, 64 (vocabulary ceiling, multi-token needles)
points.targets: Phase 4 (payoff phase, bet A weak form); Open Q#3

points.title: Open Q#1's quantization probe measures activations when the risk lives in the Jacobian
points.claim: Open Q#1 elevates quant drift to the primary risk (correct — the default stack is GGUF/Ollama) and proposes the cheap probe: 'compare fp16 vs quantized HIDDEN STATES on one model via a llama.cpp debug build' (lines 303-309). But the J-lens is a Jacobian, ∂h_final/∂h_ℓ; Q4 quantization perturbs WEIGHTS, which changes the local linear map directly, even when forward activations stay cosine-close (activations are empirically robust to Q4; the down-projection maps the workspace reads from are less so). Comparing forward hidden states systematically UNDER-measures J-lens drift. The correct probe compares the VJP/Jacobian directions themselves under fp16 vs Q4, not the activations. As written, the cheap measurement for the roadmap's #1 risk is confounded toward a falsely optimistic 'drift is small' answer.
points.evidence: roadmap lines 303-309 (Open Q#1 probe = compare hidden states); review line 79 (elevate quant drift to primary)
points.targets: Open Q#1 / Phase 0 quant sub-probe; decision-point #5 (which fp16 model)

points.title: Concede the real upside: bet B is mechanistically correct and the integration seams are code-verified — but the review's empirical baseline is not in-repo
points.claim: Bet B is well-founded and needs no workspace: the residual stream's anisotropy (a few high-variance outlier/massive-activation dimensions dominate) genuinely mis-metricizes cosine-on-BGE-M3, so Mahalanobis + Ledoit-Wolf whitening is the correct fix, with no query-time internals. The 'bounded, prepaid downside' claim also holds at the code level: fusion.py:104 makes a jspace tier zero-weight-disable cleanly (Phase 3 plugs in honestly); know_calibration.py:64 already ships a 5-feature logistic (DEFAULT_BETAS is 6-long = intercept+5), so Phase 1's 'density as the 6th feature' is exact; interference_trim at cymatics.py:526 is a real Phase-4 hook; and the splice floor bug is real (context_manager.py:1707 target=1000 → headroom_bridge.py:227 content[:1000].strip() on the default no-headroom stack). Caveat for calibration: the review's empirical grounding leans on docs/benchmarks/2026-07-06-sike-run2-fts-depth-fusion.md and the faithfulness runbook, but those filenames appear ONLY inside the review itself — not committed anywhere in the repo — so the 'RRF 0.72 vs additive 0.58' baseline that Phase 0/3 must beat is not independently reproducible from repo state (the harness scaffolding, scripts/bench_chain/s3_fts_depth_sweep.py, does exist).
points.evidence: fusion.py:76,104; know_calibration.py:64 (DEFAULT_BETAS 6-long); cymatics.py:526 interference_trim; context_manager.py:1707 + headroom_bridge.py:222-227; grep: the two cited 2026-07-06 benchmark docs resolve only inside the review file; scripts/bench_chain/s3_fts_depth_sweep present
points.targets: Phase 1 / bet B (lead recommendation); Phase 3 fusion integration; decision-point #2 (lead with bet B) and #4 (verify LatentAudit/UWSH before load-bearing)

verdict_one_line: Worth pursuing — the cheapest wins (RRF-as-default, Mahalanobis whitening of BGE-M3, density-as-6th-know-feature) are bet-independent, already 80% prepaid in shipped plumbing, and the expensive J-space bet is cheaply falsifiable at a correctly-scoped Phase 0; lead with those and the downside is bounded.

points.title: Phase 3's jspace tier is a one-liner into already-shipped, scale-safe fusion
points.claim: The roadmap's Phase 3 integration `fuser.add_tier("jspace", ranked, weight=cfg.retrieval.jspace_weight)` is API-accurate against the shipped `Fuser` — a pure, SQL-free, rank-scale-invariant data structure where `weight==0.0` is a silent no-op disable. Because RRF ranks are scale-invariant (Cormack k=60), a new geometry tier cannot destabilize the fused score the way a mis-scaled additive tier can; you A/B it by tuning one weight up from zero, and it degrades to current behavior at weight 0. This is the strongest structural reason the roadmap's ordering is honest: the production plumbing for the retrieval half of bet A already exists and is tested.
points.evidence: helix_context/retrieval/fusion.py: add_tier(tier_name, ranked_ids, weight) signature + zero-weight short-circuit (lines 104-108); knowledge_store.py branches on _fusion_mode, Fuser queried only when 'rrf' (line ~2146)
points.targets: Phase 3; Decision point 2 (lead-with-B / cheap integration)

points.title: Density-as-6th-feature (Phase 1) reuses a proven extension mechanism, not a hope
points.claim: know_calibration.py is a 5-feature logistic (N_FEATURES=5, DEFAULT_BETAS is a 6-tuple = intercept+5) and Stage 7 already demonstrated the exact incremental-feature-add the roadmap wants: freshness_min was bolted on as the 5th feature behind a `len(betas) >= 6` guard with a documented STAGE-7-EXT migration and a hand-rolled `fit_betas_from_features`. So Phase 1's 'ship density as a 6th logistic feature before any pure density gate' plugs into a working, extensible calibration surface that is already the target of live work (#239). Leading with bet B is de-risked by this.

points.title: The Phase-0 gold set is ~80% prepaid, and its miss-decomposition bounds bet A's addressable ceiling
points.claim: The roadmap says 'Helix has no gold retrieval set'; in fact the SIKE resolutions doc already carries 50 needles x 3 beds = 150 (query, gold_source) labels with per-needle gold FTS ranks and hit/miss booleans, plus computed know-confidence AUC. Crucially for IR: xl's 18 misses split into 7 pool-starvation (gold never enters retrieval — FTS ranks 63-158 or absent because 'helix' matches ~36k genes) and 11 in-pool rank-squeeze (gold at rank 7-42, evicted by echoes/dupes). A better *metric* (residual geometry / Mahalanobis) can only rescue the ~11 in-pool cases; the 7 out-of-pool ones need candidate depth, not geometry. So Phase 0's addressable ceiling on this bench is ~11/50 and its bar must be measured at fixed candidate depth — which the roadmap correctly specifies ('beats the full RRF stack at equal candidate depth'). This makes the kill criterion concrete in a way the review leaves abstract.
points.evidence: docs/benchmarks/2026-07-05-sike-bedsweep-issue-resolutions.md §5 A4 (pool starvation, FTS ranks), B2 (11/18 rank 7-42 squeeze-outs), A5 (hit/miss AUC xl 0.352); roadmap Phase 0 'equal candidate depth' bar
points.targets: Phase 0 (eval harness + pre-registered bar)

points.title: Additive doesn't just weaken the baseline — it mis-scales BGE-M3 itself, so 'beat RRF' is the honest bar and RRF-default is a free prerequisite win
points.claim: knowledge_store.py defaults fusion_mode='additive' with semantic_dense_additive_weight=16.0 while FTS bm25 is capped at 6.0. Under additive, dense cosine in [-1,1] scaled by 16 can swamp a 6.0-capped lexical head — the fusion is scale-broken in both directions (dense over-weight AND lexical head-flattening). RRF (rank-invariant) removes both pathologies, which is the mechanistic backing for the review's Run-2 claim (RRF 0.72 vs additive 0.58). The council should treat 'flip fusion_mode default to rrf' as the cheapest bet-independent IR win and the correct Phase-0 baseline — comparing residual geometry against additive would flatter it against a strawman that handicaps BGE-M3.
points.evidence: knowledge_store.py:518 fusion_mode='additive', :550 semantic_dense_additive_weight=16.0, :1725 'genome caps it at 6.0'; SIKE doc §5 B2 'FTS5 additive cap 6.0 flattens the head while tag_exact is uncapped -> A/B additive vs RRF'
points.targets: RRF vs additive baseline; Decision point 2/3

points.title: Phase 4's splice-seam plumbing already exists (foveated_caps) — cheaper than the 'M-L' estimate once the 1000-char cut is fixed

A J-coverage objective (Phase 4) is precisely a function that populates that per-idx cap array from splat-mixture coverage instead of rank. So the per-document budget-allocation plumbing the roadmap wants at the Stage-4/5 seam is already call-local and wired; Phase 4 swaps the cap-computation policy rather than building new threading. Honest caveat: it's gated behind the BROAD tier today and compress_text still truncates, so the review's 'fix the splice bug first' stands.

points.title: Cosine->Mahalanobis whitening of BGE-M3 is the single cheapest real upside — zero model internals, valid even on a total J-space kill
points.claim: The most defensible IR win in the whole plan needs no residual stream at all: BGE-M3 cosine on embedding_dense_v2 assumes isotropy, but dense embedding spaces are empirically anisotropic (a few high-variance directions dominate), a documented retrieval pathology fixed by whitening (BERT-whitening/flow line of work). A corpus covariance over the existing embedding_dense_v2 column + Ledoit-Wolf shrinkage yields a whitened Mahalanobis re-metric offline — the same covariance Phase 1's splat fit computes anyway. This is the review's rec #1 and the honest steelman: it isolates 'is anisotropy hurting retrieval?' from 'does the decoder's geometry help?', it's the natural first experiment, and it survives a full J-space bet failure. Caveat the council should log: the specific Run-2 numbers (RRF 0.72/additive 0.58) and the faithfulness runbook the review leans on are NOT committed on this branch (2026-07-06-sike-run2-*.md absent) — the mechanism is verifiable in code, the exact figures are not yet reproducible here.
points.evidence: knowledge_store.py stores embedding_dense_v2 (dense BGE-M3 cosine tier); review rec #1 (cosine->Mahalanobis whitening, bet-A-independent) + Phase 1 Ledoit-Wolf shrinkage; verified absence: docs/benchmarks/ has no 2026-07-06-sike-run2 or faithfulness-runbook file on this branch
points.targets: Bet B / Phase 1 (bet-A-independent whitening); Decision point 2

verdict_one_line: The binding constraint on our own bed is a query-agnostic Stage-4 splice cut, not retrieval geometry — so J-space Phase 0 optimizes the wrong stage and its kill-switch fires on a corpus/metric that hasn't earned the right to pre-register an nDCG bar.

points.title: The 28-point content→body gap is splice, not geometry — Phase 0 mis-targets the stage
points.claim: On the decontaminated Run-1 xl bed, RRF already gets the answer INTO the retrieved gene 72% of the time (content_has_answer=0.72) but it survives to the emitted body only 44% (body_has_answer=0.44). A better retrieval metric (J-space/Mahalanobis) can only lift the already-retrieved 0.72 ceiling; it does nothing for the 28-point loss between retrieval and emission. The whole point of bet A is upstream of where the answers are actually dying.
points.evidence: benchmarks/results/sike_fts_depth_sweep_xl.json cells: rrf content_has_answer_rate=0.72 vs body_has_answer_rate=0.44 (all depths). Root cause is a fixed query-agnostic cut: context_manager.py:1706 `target = 1000` feeding compress_text, with `complement` never emitted (roadmap review §72, B3 delivered-but-unanswerable cluster: 13 needles ≤2/30).

points.title: Run-2 proves candidate depth is not binding — a re-ranking metric fights a saturated pool
points.claim: Deepening the FTS pool 48→500 leaves RRF content_has_answer FLAT at 0.72 and DEGRADES gold_delivered 0.74→0.72→0.70. So the missing golds aren't a pooling problem a better metric reorders — the worst misses are lexically absent from the top-200 entirely. J-space/Mahalanobis re-ranks in-pool candidates; it does not recall what FTS never surfaces. The addressable slice (in-pool rank-7–42 squeeze) is exactly what RRF already rescues via rank-fusion, immune to the additive 6.0 cap.

A4: helix_expression_budget/pipeline_steps absent from top-200 ('helix' matches ~36k genes). Fuser rank-fusion in retrieval/fusion.py:76.

points.title: Phase 0's pre-registered nDCG@10 kill-switch is uncomputable on the eval set that exists
points.claim: Phase 0 pre-registers 'beats BGE-M3 by ≥5 points Recall@10 / nDCG@10.' But the SIKE 50-needle harness is binary any-of source-id substring matching with no graded relevance judgments — nDCG needs graded labels the corpus doesn't have, and 4/63 gold paths are stale. The bar can't fire honestly as written. points.title: Phase 4 J-coverage duplicates an existing resonance selector and inherits the splice confound
points.claim: The coverage-shaped selector Phase 4 proposes to build in the Stage-4/5 seam already exists as interference_trim: a query-resonance fragment selector over the 256-bin cymatics spectrum with a splice_aggressiveness threshold and min_codons_kept floor. Swapping its resonance metric for J-coverage doesn't change that its downstream compress step still caps each fragment at 1000 query-agnostic chars. Phase 4's kill-switch ('no end-to-end QA gain at fixed budget') WILL fire — but as a false-negative on the coverage hypothesis, confounded by the splice bug it sits on top of.

points.title: Bet B density know/go is fit on an anti-correlated signal and is structurally blind to our actual failure
points.claim: The current know-confidence is worse than random (misses score HIGHER than hits, AUC 0.352). Adding a 6th Mahalanobis-density logistic feature does not fix a sign-inverted signal. And Mahalanobis density measures adherence to the corpus distribution — so the 260 echo genes (verbatim prior query/response, source_id NULL, top-8 FTS for helix_headroom_port) are maximally in-distribution and score LOW anomaly / HIGH confidence, actively reinforcing the anti-signal. Phase 1's 'density dominates the risk-coverage curve' validation cannot be trusted until know-AUC>0.7 (Run-3 gate) passes AND provenance/anti-poisoning is paired in.

points.title: Conceded upside — Mahalanobis whitening — is real but gated on backfill + shrinkage + condition number
points.claim: The one honest bet-A-independent win is cosine→Mahalanobis whitening of BGE-M3: anisotropy is real and whitening is cheap (d×d Cholesky, ~1M flops/query at d=1024). But it is under-priced: (a) embedding_dense_v2 is NULL until backfill runs, so the corpus covariance can't even be estimated yet; (b) a 1024×1024 covariance needs n≫1024 non-null vectors + Ledoit-Wolf, or whitening amplifies low-variance noise directions and can HURT ranking; (c) it only re-ranks in-pool candidates, so it inherits the depth-invariant ceiling from point 2. Upside is bounded above by the splice gap.

Same Ledoit-Wolf singularity the roadmap flags for Phase 1 (L106-109).

specialty: Density modeling / applied statistics (Gaussian-splat genes, Mahalanobis under anisotropy, Ledoit-Wolf shrinkage, density know/go calibration)

points.title: Bank the anisotropy win with ONE global Σ before building any per-gene splats
points.claim: The highest-certainty, cheapest slice of Bet B is a single corpus-level Ledoit-Wolf-shrunk whitening transform W fit offline over the already-existing genes.embedding_dense_v2 column and applied to BOTH query and gene vectors at query time — turning cosine into a global Mahalanobis re-metric with zero per-gene degeneracy risk. It captures most of the anisotropy correction the review credits to Bet B while entirely side-stepping the sample-starvation failure mode, and de-risks Phase 1 by answering 'does whitening alone move retrieval?' before the splats table exists. Ship it as Phase 1 step 0, storing W in the proposed spaces.basis/center — one offline pass, no new schema beyond a single spaces row.

So the review's 'Mahalanobis whitening available now' is genuinely ~50 lines of new offline code — cheap, but not existing.

points.title: Phase 1's 'better-calibrated-than-the-logistic' bar is softer than stated — the logistic was never fit
points.claim: Phase 1's kill criterion ('density no better calibrated than the current logistic') races an UNCALIBRATED baseline. The density gate isn't beating a tuned model — both share the same missing prerequisite: a labeled (query, relevant-gene) reliability set. Correct sequencing: build the Phase-0/SIKE-derived eval harness, REFIT the logistic as the real baseline, THEN compare density on risk-coverage/ECE. Comparing density against the shipped hand-set defaults is an unfair-easy win that will not survive a properly-fit logistic; flag it so Phase 1 doesn't book a false positive that later reverses.

The betas are priors, never regressed on data.

points.title: Sample starvation is the COMMON case, not a tail risk — make rank adaptive to n_obs, don't pin rank=16
points.claim: Per-gene Σ must be pooled from a parent gene's child-fragment embeddings (linked via the codons list), and n_obs = chunk_count. For the many small docs in a code/notes corpus that is 1-3 chunks -> a rank-<=2 scatter in 1024-d. At the proposed [splat] rank=16, effective factor rank is min(16, n_obs-1), so those genes collapse to mu + diagonal ~= scaled cosine — the roadmap's own 'covariances collapse to the prior' failure, but as the majority case. Fix that keeps the upside: make k adaptive (k_eff=min(rank, n_obs-1)) and let Ledoit-Wolf shrinkage intensity lambda->1 carry low-n genes to the global prior. Pre-register: MEASURE the chunk_count distribution over genome.db before fixing rank — the config default should follow the data, not precede it. A static rank=16 is in tension with the shrinkage='ledoit_wolf' the same config names.
points.evidence: knowledge_store.py:3651 reassemble() reads parent.codons (ordered child gene_ids; is_parent=true marker); each child is its own gene row carrying embedding_dense_v2; roadmap Phase 1 config '[splat] rank=16, shrinkage=ledoit_wolf, min_chunks=2' plus its own Risk block 'covariances collapse toward the shared prior'.
points.targets: Phase 1 covariance fit + [splat] config defaults; roadmap Phase 1 risk register

points.title: Mixture log-density over top-K is the fragile feature — normalize with a shared Σ, keep only μ per-gene
points.claim: The roadmap adds 'mixture log-density over top-K' as a know feature. In 1024-d a Gaussian log-density's log-det(Σ) term swings enormously when Σ is estimated from 2-3 samples: a near-singular per-gene Σ makes the density dominated by its normalization constant, not the query's proximity — so the mixture-density feature is MORE exposed to sample starvation than a plain Mahalanobis-to-top1. Robust cheapest design: use the SHARED shrunk global Σ for the density normalization (log-det constant across genes, cancels in cross-gene comparison) and let only μ be per-gene. That 'μ-per-gene + global-Σ' splat is well-conditioned by construction, O(dk) via one Woodbury factorization, and is exactly the limit low-n genes fall into anyway — so make it the DEFAULT and promote to a full per-gene Σ only where n_obs and MEASURED inter-gene covariance divergence justify it (invert the roadmap's detect-then-fallback into detect-then-upgrade).
points.evidence: roadmap Phase 1 'add per-candidate log-density and mixture log-density over top-K as features' and its 'Woodbury identity makes Mahalanobis O(dk)' claim; roadmap Phase 1 Risk 'covariances collapse... Detect by measuring inter-gene covariance divergence'.
points.targets: Phase 1 know-feature integration; Phase 1 risk/fallback design

points.title: Density's real contribution is an ABSOLUTE novelty signal the all-relative logistic structurally cannot express — and it complements, not replaces, poisoning defense
points.claim: Every feature in the current logistic is RELATIVE: tanh(top_score/s_ref), score_gap (top1-top2), lexical_dense_agree, coordinate_confidence, freshness_min. None is an absolute anchor, so the logistic cannot detect a confident retrieval of an OOD query — a query far from ALL gene modes can still have high top_score AND a large top1-top2 gap. Mahalanobis distance to the top gene's splat IS that absolute anchor (the LatentAudit 'know-vs-go' signal), so density earns nonzero beta as an orthogonal OOD/novelty dimension even if it doesn't dominate ranking. Concede the review's Risk #1 precisely: Mahalanobis catches OOD queries but is blind to in-distribution POISON (a fluent false doc sits at high density), so density and provenance/CWoLa are complementary — density closes the OOD hole, provenance closes the poison hole. Ship density as the '+1 logistic feature first' path, keep the anti-poisoning orthogonal.
points.evidence: know_calibration.py:267-278 compute_confidence — all five z-terms are relative/agreement signals, no absolute-distance term; review New-Risk #1 'density gate is blind to corpus poisoning... must be paired with provenance/anti-poisoning'; roadmap Phase 1 'first ship density as a 6th logistic feature... only replace with a pure density threshold if it wins head-to-head'.
points.targets: Phase 1 know-feature integration; review New-Risk #1; #239

specialty: Density modeling / applied statistics (Gaussian-splat genes, Mahalanobis vs cosine under anisotropy, Ledoit-Wolf shrinkage, density know/go calibration)
verdict_one_line: Bet B's Gaussian-splat density gate is sample-starved by construction and largely re-expresses signals the current logistic already carries; its first kill-switch (covariance collapse to the shared prior) fires on nearly every real corpus — and the honest cheap win it's being credited for is just calibrating the incumbent logistic, which has never been calibrated at all.

points.title: Per-gene covariance is sample-starved by construction — the Phase-1 kill-switch is the expected case, not a tail risk
points.claim: Phase 1's own named failure ('covariances collapse toward the shared prior, Mahalanobis becomes scaled cosine') is not a risk to monitor, it is the default outcome. A gene's Sigma is fit from its chunk/fragment embeddings (n_obs), and no document yields anywhere near d=1024 chunks; for n_obs << d, Ledoit-Wolf's optimal shrinkage intensity delta* drives toward 1, so every gene's Sigma approximates the single corpus prior. A shared Sigma across genes is one global whitening — literally 'scaled cosine.' The per-gene anisotropy that justifies the entire splats table then carries ~0 information, and the roadmap's own detector ('measure inter-gene covariance divergence; if low, drop the density gate') triggers on almost any corpus. Concession: Ledoit-Wolf + corpus prior is the correct standard fix and the roadmap named it right — but that fix is exactly what guarantees near-degenerate per-gene Sigmas here.
points.evidence: knowledge_store.py:1361 stores one gene-level embedding_dense_v2 (BGE-M3, 1024-d) per gene; roadmap Phase 1 rank=16 means F is 1024x16=16,384 params + 1024 diag fit from n_obs chunks; SIKE beds are 46,517 / 15,889 / 80,363 genes of short needles (docs/benchmarks/2026-07-05-sike-bedsweep-issue-resolutions.md sec.1).
points.targets: Phase 1 (bet B) — kill-switch 'covariance collapse to shared prior'; this is the FIRST kill-switch that fires for bet B.

points.title: 'Fit Sigma from already-computed chunk embeddings' is false — chunk vectors are discarded, so Phase 1 hides a full re-embedding backfill
points.claim: Phase 1 asserts mu and Sigma are 'fitted from the gene's chunk/fragment embeddings (already computed).' The schema persists only ONE gene-level dense vector, pooled from gene.content; individual chunk embeddings are thrown away at ingest. Fitting Sigma therefore requires re-embedding every chunk of every gene — a full BGE-M3 pass over 46k-80k genes per bed, the same cost class as the existing backfill_bgem3_v2.py, not a free by-product. The 'M effort / known-how' label understates the work and the GPU time.
points.evidence: knowledge_store.py:1361-1363 computes _encode_dense_v2_blob(gene.content, ...) — a single vector from content; no chunk-embedding table exists (grep for splat/chunk_embed returns nothing); CLAUDE.md gotcha confirms even the gene-level vector needs scripts/backfill_bgem3_v2.py.
points.targets: Phase 1 schema/backfill (splats table, backfill_splats.py) and its effort estimate.

points.title: Whitening BGE-M3 is a category error — the review's 'win available now' imports anisotropy from raw residual streams into a cosine-trained sphere
points.claim: The review's flagship bet-B-independent win — 'cosine->Mahalanobis whitening of BGE-M3 similarity, available now' — borrows the anisotropy argument from RAW transformer residual streams (LatentAudit on Llama-3-8B hidden states). BGE-M3 is a contrastively-trained, L2-normalized retrieval embedding explicitly optimized (InfoNCE/temperature) so that cosine is the correct metric on the unit sphere. The high-variance task-agnostic directions that make cosine wrong for residual streams are precisely what contrastive normalization trains out. Whitening such a space is as likely to amplify low-variance noise directions as to help; this is the least-grounded of the 'safe' wins. Concession: BERT-style embeddings have shown whitening gains, so it is worth a one-hour ablation — but it should not be pre-registered as a near-certain win.
points.evidence: review lines 35/52/77 (Mahalanobis import + Recommendation 1); BGE-M3 dense is 1024-d normalized (CLAUDE.md); helix.toml:272 already defaults cymatics distance_metric to 'cosine'.
points.targets: Review Recommendation 1 / Decision point 2 ('lead with bet B + Mahalanobis whitening').

points.title: The density feature is collinear with score_gap — the 'logistic can't express between-mode ambiguity' claim is largely false
points.claim: Phase 1's headline justification is a failure 'the scalar logistic can't express: queries landing between two gene modes (high top_score, high ambiguity) should now abstain.' But the logistic's second feature IS score_gap = top1-top2 with beta=1.5 — already a high-top-score-yet-ambiguous detector. The per-candidate/mixture log-density feature is therefore substantially collinear with an existing feature; adding it as a 6th->7th beta to a logistic fit on ~1000 labels inflates coefficient variance (identifiability), not calibration quality. The kill-switch 'density no better calibrated than logistic' fires — for a collinearity/statistical-power reason, not because the density math is wrong, so the phase 'fails' without teaching you anything. Concession: a query landing between two modes of a single MULTIMODAL gene's chunk distribution is a genuine geometry score_gap does not capture — but point 1 shows that within-gene multimodality is exactly what shrinkage erases.
points.evidence: know_calibration.py:64 DEFAULT_BETAS and :268-269 (tanh(score_gap/g_ref) as feature 2, beta2=1.5); roadmap Phase 1 validation lines 130-132.
points.targets: Phase 1 validation / 'ship density as a 6th logistic feature' path.

points.title: Mahalanobis measures typicality, not truth — it green-lights self-consistent poison and optimizes against the wrong SIKE target
points.claim: Density know/go scores a query by how typical it is w.r.t. the retrieved gene's own scatter. Echo-contaminated genes (Helix's SIKE poisoning failure) have internally self-consistent chunks, so they yield LOW Mahalanobis distance -> HIGH density confidence — inverting the desired signal. The valid SIKE run already logs 7/14/15 false-positive needles per bed (marked delivered, yet 0/10 consumers correct); a density gate would raise, not lower, confidence on precisely these. The deeper statistical point (beyond the review's generic poisoning note): density AUROC is trained against adherence/typicality, which is orthogonal to retrieval correctness, so the blueprint's cited 0.942 AUROC (a hallucination/adherence task) does not transfer to the know/go target Phase 1 validates on.
points.evidence: docs/benchmarks/2026-07-05-sike-bedsweep-issue-resolutions.md sec.1 (7/14/15 false-positive needles; echo contamination fixed via HELIX_DISABLE_LEARN); review lines 35 and 63.
points.targets: Phase 1 success metric (risk-coverage/ECE) + risk register (poisoning-blind density).

points.title: The incumbent baseline is uncalibrated — fix that (cheap, #239) before betting on density; and density pulls numpy/scipy into a pure-math hot path
points.claim: Phase 1 measures density against 'the current logistic,' but helix.toml ships the spec DEFAULT betas and the fit target the calibration script names, benchmarks/located_n1000.py, does not exist (only a shell wrapper _run_n1000_located.sh). The incumbent has NEVER been calibrated on Helix's retriever. So the honest experiment must calibrate BOTH variants on the same missing ~500-1000 labels — and simply calibrating the existing 5-feature logistic first (the actual #239 live work) is the cheap, high-probability win that density is being pre-credited for. Separately, the know path is pure-Python (imports only math); Mahalanobis/Woodbury/Ledoit-Wolf/eigendecomposition drag numpy (optional/dev extra, not core) plus scipy/sklearn (not dependencies at all) into the request hot path — against the 'algorithmic, no inference at query time' ethos.
points.evidence: helix.toml:449 betas=[-2.0,2.0,1.5,0.7,1.8,1.5] (= DEFAULT_BETAS, know_calibration.py:64) with no calibrated_at set; scripts/calibrate_know_confidence.py names benchmarks/located_n1000.py which is absent; pyproject.toml:44/86/92 list numpy only as embeddings/dev extras; know_calibration.py imports math only.
points.targets: Phase 1 baseline definition, #239 (know/miss recalibration), Decision point 2.

specialty: Density modeling / applied statistics (Gaussian-splat genes, Mahalanobis vs cosine under anisotropy, covariance shrinkage & singularity, density know/go calibration)
verdict_one_line: Bet B's genuinely-prepaid win is a single corpus-level whitening transform (the repo already has hard evidence it's needed), but Phase 1 as specced builds per-gene Gaussian densities on unit-sphere data with 3-16 samples in 1024-d — a mis-specified, unidentifiable estimator — and grades itself against a logistic that was never actually calibrated.

points.title: Phase 1 fits a Gaussian density on the unit sphere — log_density is mis-specified
points.claim: Every stored embedding_dense_v2 is L2-normalized, so all chunk embeddings live on S^1023. Fitting mu + Sigma=FF^T+D as a full-space Gaussian and emitting a KnowBlock 'log_density' / 'mixture log-density over top-K' (Phase 1 schema) is a model mismatch: the data has zero radial extent, so Sigma is intrinsically rank-deficient in the mean direction and the Gaussian normalizing constant is meaningless. Mahalanobis-as-whitened-cosine is a valid *metric*; a calibrated *density* on this data needs a von Mises-Fisher mixture or a tangent-space Gaussian at mu. Recommend dropping the literal log_density feature or replacing it with a vMF concentration / tangent-space score.
points.evidence: bgem3_codec.py:141-144 and :185-189 (per-row L2 renormalize on both encode and encode_batch); roadmap Phase 1 lines 99-122 (Sigma=FF^T+D, log_density/mahalanobis_top1 KnowBlock fields)
points.targets: Bet B / Phase 1 (schema: log_density, mahalanobis_top1 fields; validation: 'mixture log-density over top-K')

points.title: Per-gene covariance is unidentifiable by parameter-counting, not a risk to 'detect'
points.claim: The roadmap treats 'covariances collapse toward the shared prior' as a post-hoc risk to measure via inter-gene divergence. It is deterministic: F is d x k = 1024 x 16 = 16,384 free parameters, and the per-gene sample scatter from N chunks has rank <= N-1. The roadmap itself concedes a 3-chunk gene gives a rank-2 scatter. At the ~2000-char passage cap, the majority of code/doc genes have N < 16 chunks, so the k=16 factor block is unidentifiable and F is 100% shrinkage prior for those genes — Mahalanobis is exactly scaled global-whitened cosine there, carrying zero per-gene information at full ~80KB/gene storage. Fix: set adaptive rank k_g = min(rank, N_obs-1), store diagonal-only for starved genes, and raise the min_chunks default well above the proposed 2 (a 2-chunk gene has a rank-1 covariance).
points.evidence: roadmap line 123 ([splat] rank=16, min_chunks=2), lines 105-110 & 134-138 (the collapse 'risk'), line 184 (~80KB/gene estimate); bgem3_codec.py:34 (PASSAGE_CHAR_CAP=2000); context_manager.py:1010,1044-1056 (chunks stored as separate is_fragment gene rows)
points.targets: Bet B / Phase 1 ([splat] rank + min_chunks defaults; Risk/fallback paragraph)

points.title: The review conflates two statistically opposite Mahalanobis objects; only the global one is the prepaid win
points.claim: The review says 'Mirror the LatentAudit recipe' for Phase 1 and calls cosine->Mahalanobis whitening 'a bet-A-independent win available now' — but LatentAudit-style gating uses a SINGLE corpus-level Sigma (Ledoit-Wolf over the whole calibration set, N>>d, well-conditioned). That is global whitening and it is sound and cheap. Phase 1's schema builds PER-GENE Sigma (N<<d, ill-conditioned — see prior point). Opposite sample regimes, different estimators. Split Phase 1: 1a = global mean-centering + whitening of BGE-M3 similarity (do now, ~1 day, stored once in the proposed spaces.center/basis), gated on nothing; 1b = per-gene splats, gated on identifiability.
points.evidence: review lines 52 & 57 (LatentAudit 'near-drop-in', whitening 'available now'); roadmap line 115 (spaces table has center/basis BLOBs already); knowledge_store.py:383-404 documents the exact anisotropy pathology that global whitening fixes
points.targets: Decision-point 2 & Recommendation 1 (lead with bet B + Mahalanobis whitening); Phase 1 scoping

points.title: The repo already has hard anisotropy evidence that makes global whitening a measured need, not a hypothesis
points.claim: Global whitening isn't speculative here. knowledge_store.py documents that the margin-over-random calibrated dense-gate threshold (0.779) sat ABOVE the entire corpus's max query-doc cosine (~0.713), so 0/5000 pool chunks cleared the gate by dense score and a 480-question ERB run had 70% never-surfaced golds. That is the textbook signature of embedding anisotropy crushing all cosines into a narrow high band — the mean-centering step of whitening directly de-biases it. This is the single strongest empirical argument in the whole roadmap for the bet-B whitening move, and neither design doc cites it.
points.evidence: knowledge_store.py:383-404 (dense_pool_floor_genes docstring: threshold 0.779 > max cosine 0.713, 0/5000, 70% never-surfaced golds); mirrored anisotropy note at config.py:379
points.targets: Bet B / Phase 1a (global whitening) and Recommendation 1

points.title: Phase 1's kill-criterion grades density against a logistic that was never calibrated, with no ECE harness in tree
points.claim: Phase 1's stated kill is 'density no better calibrated than the logistic.' But the shipped logistic has never been fit: helix.toml betas are byte-identical to DEFAULT_BETAS, no calibrated_at is set, and benchmarks/located_n1000.py (the calibration-data generator both the script and helix.toml reference) does not exist. No ECE / risk-coverage code exists anywhere (grep hits only docs). So the baseline is a hand-picked prior and the risk-coverage/ECE curves Phase 1 promises must be built from scratch first. Cheapest honest first step, before any splat schema: build the labeled set + ECE harness, actually fit the existing 5-feature logistic, and measure how much of the gap a *fit* logistic closes — it is the shared prerequisite for evaluating density anyway.
points.evidence: helix.toml:449 betas=[-2.0,2.0,1.5,0.7,1.8,1.5] == know_calibration.py:64 DEFAULT_BETAS, no calibrated_at value present; calibrate_know_confidence.py:12-20 references missing benchmarks/located_n1000.py; grep ECE -> docs only
points.targets: Phase 1 validation & kill-criterion; the 'eval harness is highest-leverage' claim (roadmap lines 60-67)

points.title: Density typicality and poison-resistance are orthogonal by construction — AND them, never OR
points.claim: Sharpening the review's risk #1: Mahalanobis/density measures typicality under the fitted corpus distribution, so a fluent poisoned document that matches corpus style scores as HIGH-density / LOW-anomaly precisely because it conforms — anomaly detection rewards conformity, and no better covariance estimator changes that. For the SIKE echo-contamination case (fixed via HELIX_DISABLE_LEARN), the echoed wrong answers would have RAISED local density around the wrong mode, making a density gate *more* confident on the poisoned corpus. Therefore any Phase-1 path where density raises KnowBlock.confidence must be conjoined (AND) with a provenance/CWoLa signal, never disjoined (OR). Also worth a nested-LRT check: density may be collinear with the existing score_gap feature, which already partly expresses the 'between two modes' abstain case the roadmap wants density to fix.
points.evidence: review line 63 (risk #1) + roadmap line 132 ('between two gene modes... should abstain'); know_calibration.py:268-271 (score_gap and lexical_dense_agree already in the logistic); commit 39685b7 (HELIX_DISABLE_LEARN echo-contamination fix)
points.targets: New-risk register (risk #1) and Phase 1 feature-integration ('6th logistic feature first')

specialty: ML systems / serving (offline hidden-state capture, quantization drift, soft-prefix serving)
verdict_one_line: Bet A survives on the default Ollama stack only if Phase 2 lands as a stored linear re-metric (not a new encoder) and Phase 4 is preceded by the cheap query-aware-splice experiment; the offline-capture and fusion plumbing are ~60% prepaid, but Phase 0's decoder-in-fp16 regime and Phase 5's proxy-to-server category change are the real, honest costs.

points.title: Phase 2's 'deflationary' linear-probe outcome is actually the win — pre-register it as primary
points.claim: The review treats 'a linear probe from BGE-M3 predicts J-coords ⇒ J-space ≈ relabeled semantics' as philosophically deflationary. From a serving standpoint it is the single best outcome: it collapses the entire query-time-internals problem (Open Q#2) into a stored d×r matrix in spaces.basis — no ONNX export, no new encoder, no <10ms budget risk — and it runs on the default Ollama/CPU stack that the roadmap otherwise says J-space 'permanently excludes'. Helix ALREADY computes the BGE-M3 query embedding on the request path via the process-shared codec (backends/bgem3_codec.py get_shared_codec at ~line 246), so Phase 2 in the linear case is a 1024×16 matmul (~16K FLOPs, nanoseconds) appended to work already done. Phase 2 should pre-register the linear probe as the primary hypothesis and the 2-layer MLP as fallback, inverting the roadmap's framing — the linear path is the ONLY thing that keeps the default stack in bet A. Honest concession: this only pays if J-coords are a near-linear image of BGE-M3 space, and if Ledoit-Wolf whitening (bet B) already absorbs the anisotropy, the marginal lift of a learned re-metric over plain whitening may be near zero.
points.evidence: backends/bgem3_codec.py (get_shared_codec, process-shared BGE-M3 already on query path); roadmap Phase 2 fallback (b) 'a d×r matrix stored in spaces.basis, no new encoder'; review Open Q#2 / decision point #2
points.targets: Bet A / Phase 2 / Open Q#2 / decision point #2

points.title: Offline-capture infra is ~60% prepaid, but Phase 0 needs the DECODER in fp16 — a memory regime nothing in the fleet loads today
points.claim: The roadmap flags 'HF fp16 + GPU, once per model profile' as acceptable; the good news it undersells is that the loading scaffolding already exists — Helix ships torch+transformers+sentence-transformers and a centralized device singleton (backends/deberta_backend.py resolves via helix_context.hardware.get_hardware().device → torch.device), so benchmarks/eval_retrieval.py can reuse real patterns, not greenfield. The sharp caveat my specialty adds: everything loaded today is an ENCODER (BGE-M3 XLM-R, DeBERTa cross-encoder) at device='cpu' default, ~2GB. Phase 0 needs the GENERATION decoder in fp16 with output_hidden_states — grep confirms output_hidden_states/hidden_states appears NOWHERE in helix_context/ — a 7-14B model = 14-28GB VRAM, a regime the default (device='cpu') stack never assumes. Worse, the decoder whose J-space matters is the proxied UPSTREAM, which Helix never loads — it forwards it over HTTP (config.py ribosome model default is only the small compressor gemma4:e2b). So decision point #5 is load-bearing: Phase 0 must pick a model Helix can both proxy for generation AND load in fp16, and they must be the same artifact.
points.evidence: grep: zero output_hidden_states/hidden_states in helix_context/; backends/deberta_backend.py imports torch + get_hardware().device; backends/bgem3_codec.py device='cpu' default; config.py:30 ribosome model='gemma4:e2b' (compressor, not the generation decoder)
points.targets: Phase 0 / decision point #5 / Open Q#1

points.title: Phase 4's payoff has a near-free first draft: make splice query-aware BEFORE building J-coverage — the compressor is currently query-blind
points.claim: Both splice call sites — context_manager.py:1707 and :2649 — call compress_text(g.content, target_chars=target, content_type=g.promoter.domains). Verified: the query is NEVER passed; Kompress/ModernBERT compresses to a ratio with zero query signal. So Helix's current 'output shape' is query-agnostic ratio compression. That means Phase 4's entire thesis ('does selection-side shaping matter', Open Q#3) has a much cheaper first experiment than a J-space coverage/facility-location objective: pass the query embedding into fragment selection at Stage 4, on the default stack, no hidden states, no new model. If query-aware splice captures the QA gain at fixed 7000-token budget, the exotic J-coverage half of Phase 4 is unnecessary; if it doesn't, that's direct evidence decoders are composition-robust and Phase 5 is the only lever. Either way it decisively de-risks Phases 4-5 for the cost of one Stage-4 change. Honest concession: query-aware splice still inherits the floor bug — Kompress's ratio compression can drop the answer, and the discarded remainder is only surfaced via g.complement fallback at :2649 (not at the :1707 primary path), so the budget-proportional-cap / emit-complement fix must land first regardless.
points.evidence: context_manager.py:1707 and :2649 both call compress_text with content+domains only, no query arg; headroom_bridge.py:191 compress_text signature has no query parameter; :2649 shows 'g.complement or compress_text(...)' fallback
points.targets: Phase 4 / Open Q#3 (text→residual controllability)

points.title: Phase 5 is a proxy→server category change, but vLLM Automatic Prefix Caching makes the review's cost model too pessimistic
points.claim: The roadmap gates Phase 5 as an 'opt-in backend profile'; the serving-specific truth is sharper — Helix is a transparent HTTP proxy in front of someone else's decoder (POST /v1/chat/completions pass-through, [server] upstream). Soft-prefix via vLLM prompt_embeds requires Helix to BE the inference engine (own the KV cache), not proxy one. That is an architectural category change, and it forfeits the legible/attributable know-miss contract that is Helix's product thesis (review risk #4). The one thing the review's validation ('count prefix vectors at their KV cost') gets wrong in Phase 5's favor: vLLM's Automatic Prefix Caching means a stable rendered splat-prefix becomes cached KV, so a repeated prefix across turns amortizes to ~zero — and that is exactly Helix's regime, since session_delivery_enabled already elides repeats across a multi-turn session. So IF Phase 5 ever runs, the amortized cost is better than 'per-turn KV cost' implies. Honest concession: this remains a model-/finetune-specific research POC that abandons the proxy thesis and the legibility contract for that profile — I'd keep it as a research escape hatch, not a product direction (decision point #3).
points.evidence: CLAUDE.md HTTP endpoints (POST /v1/chat/completions OpenAI-compatible proxy; [server] upstream); roadmap Phase 5 flagged constraints (vLLM prompt-embeds yes, Ollama no); review risk #4 legibility; session_delivery_enabled repeat-elision (CLAUDE.md [budget])
points.targets: Phase 5 / decision point #3

points.title: Quantization drift (Open Q#1) is only HALF cheaply answerable — final-layer embeddings exist without a fork, mid-layer Workspace Band does not
points.claim: The roadmap proposes measuring fp16-vs-Q4 drift 'via a llama.cpp debug build' (a fork). Cheaper existing surface my specialty flags: Ollama /api/embeddings and llama.cpp --embedding already expose the FINAL-layer residual of the served Q4 artifact with no fork — enough to test whether the deployed GGUF's final-layer geometry tracks its fp16 parent, the cheapest possible 'is per-model actually per-artifact' probe. BUT the Anthropic Workspace Band the review anchors on is MID-layer (median ~25 active J-lens vectors plateauing in mid layers), and neither Ollama nor stock llama.cpp exposes mid-layer states. So the deployed artifact's mid-layer drift — the layers that actually carry the J-lens — stays unmeasured without the fork, and Phase 0's Jacobian probe genuinely requires HF fp16. Net: Open Q#1 splits cleanly — final-layer drift is measurable now for near-zero cost and worth doing first as a go/no-go smoke test; mid-layer drift (the load-bearing half) is fork-gated, which raises the true cost of treating drift as 'measurable in Phase 0 at small cost'. This determines whether Phase 3 profile management is a one-off or a per-artifact treadmill.
points.evidence: grep: no /api/embeddings or --embedding reference in helix_context/ (surface is unused today, so this is net-new but cheap); review 'J-space is mid-layer Workspace Band, median ~25 vectors'; roadmap Open Q#1 + cross-phase 'GGUF-quantized with no fp16 counterpart cannot get a J-space profile without a llama.cpp fork'
points.targets: Open Q#1 (quant drift) / Phase 0 / Phase 3 profile management / decision point #5

specialty: ML systems / serving (offline hidden-state capture, fp16-vs-Q4 quantization drift, GGUF/Ollama hidden-state constraint, soft-prefix / prompt_embeds serving)
verdict_one_line: Phases 2 and 5 are the roadmap's weakest bets: Phase 2's only winning branch produces the same d×r re-metric the review already recommends getting cheaply from bet B, and Phase 5 calibrates soft prefixes on an fp16 model the default GGUF/Ollama stack never runs — both should be cut or hard-gated behind bet B, not sequenced as a research program.

points.title: Phase 2 is dominated in both branches — its winning case proves it was unnecessary
points.claim: Phase 2's premise is 'map the BGE-M3 query embedding to J-coords with a CPU-cheap probe.' If a LINEAR probe hits the τ≥0.8 bar, the roadmap's own fallback (b) admits J-space is 'a learned re-metric on the existing embedding — a d×r matrix in spaces.basis, no new encoder' — which is functionally identical to the cosine→Mahalanobis/Ledoit-Wolf whitening the review recommends leading with (a d×r whitening matrix on BGE-M3, query-time-cheap, requiring NO fp16 decoder capture). If the probe must be NONLINEAR to work, it is exactly the case the roadmap says 'collapses OOD' and fails its own bar. So Phase 2 either reduces to bet B's whitening (already available now, no Phase 0 GPU capture) or fails the OOD kill-switch. It is a research detour whose success condition is 'we didn't need it.' Cut Phase 2; ship the whitening in Phase 1.
points.evidence: roadmap L140-171 (Phase 2 goal + fallback b: 'd×r matrix stored in spaces.basis'); Open Q#2 L313-317 ('if yes via a linear probe... J-space ≈ relabeled semantics'); review L52 + L77 ('cosine→Mahalanobis whitening of BGE-M3 is a bet-A-independent win available now'). knowledge_store.py+config.py confirm BGE-M3 query embedding already computed at query time; no jproj/onnx artifact exists in repo.
points.targets: Phase 2 (bet A); decision-point #2 (lead with bet B + Mahalanobis whitening)

points.title: Phase 5 kill-switch fires at fp16-vs-Q4 drift: you cannot calibrate on one model and serve on another
points.claim: Offline capture runs on fp16/bf16 HF weights on GPU (roadmap is explicit: 'NOT Ollama... requires fp16/bf16 weights and a GPU'), but the deployed decoder on Helix's default stack is Q4 GGUF via Ollama. A soft prefix is a continuous residual-stream vector whose attention dot-products depend on exactly the per-channel activation scales that Q4 quantization perturbs — it is the single most quantization-fragile artifact you can ship. A prefix tuned on the fp16 parent is out-of-distribution for the Q4 child, and Ollama/llama.cpp cannot ingest embedding-level input at all ('no without a fork'). The stack that accepts soft prefixes (vLLM/HF fp16) is not the stack Helix fronts (Ollama Q4), and there is no bridge. The roadmap treats this (Open Q#1) as 'measurable in Phase 0 at small cost'; it is not a cost item, it is the coherence question for the entire phase, and the honest expected answer on the default stack is 'incoherent.'
points.evidence: roadmap L69-71 (fp16/GPU capture, not Ollama), L257-264 (stack gate: llama.cpp/Ollama 'no'; 'soft prompts are model- and even finetune-specific; every upstream swap invalidates the renderer'), L283-289 + Open Q#1 L303-309. config.py:66 _LOCAL_BACKENDS=('ollama','deberta'); CLAUDE.md default upstream = Ollama. Grep: output_hidden_states / prompt_embeds / inputs_embeds occur in ZERO code files (only the two design docs).
points.targets: Phase 5 (strong form, bet A); Open Q#1; decision-point #5 (which single fp16 model)

points.title: Phase 5 has no serving-cost headroom — its own 'matched effective budget' rule is the proof, and prompt_embeds break prefix caching
points.claim: The Phase 5 validation counts 'prefix vectors at their KV cost.' That accounting is fair and it is fatal: N prefix embedding vectors produce N KV-cache entries at the same attention FLOPs as N text tokens, so soft-prefix only wins if it packs many text tokens into far fewer vectors — but the review's own cited literature (attention collapse, prompt waywardness, PrefixMemory-Tuning) caps how long a coherent soft prefix can be, so you cannot scale the compression ratio up. Worse for serving: vLLM Automatic Prefix Caching — the review's cited efficiency lever — hashes token-ID blocks; prompt_embeds bypass tokenization and generally cannot be reused by APC, so you forfeit prefix caching precisely on the injected-context prefix. Add per-finetune renderer invalidation on every upstream swap and Phase 5's serving TCO is worse than text injection, not better, on the exact stack (vLLM) that could run it.
points.evidence: roadmap L273-275 ('matched effective budget (count prefix vectors at their KV cost)'), L261-264 (per-finetune invalidation). review L37 (PrefixMemory-Tuning to 'dodge attention collapse', 'vLLM prompt_embeds + Automatic Prefix Caching'), L59 (prompt waywardness, un-decodable manifold). No KV/serving cost model exists in repo (telemetry/otel.py instruments encoder stages only).
points.targets: Phase 5; decision-point #3 (is Phase 5 off the table)

points.title: The offline decoder-capture pipeline that Phases 0/2/3/5 all assume does not exist and the hardware layer is mis-sized for it
points.claim: Every torch model in helix_context is an ENCODER used for embedding/rerank/NLI (bgem3_codec, splade_backend, deberta_backend, nli_backend, sema, tagger) — grep confirms zero occurrences of output_hidden_states on the decoder being proxied anywhere in code. Phases 0/2/3/5 require a net-new, genome-scale forward-pass-and-dump pipeline over an fp16 decoder, and the hardware batch-size table tops out at 24GB-VRAM tiers sized for small encoders (rerank/splice/splade/nli, batch 32-128) with no row for a 7B+ fp16 decoder (~14GB weights) plus per-layer activation capture across a {25/50/75%} sweep over 46k-80k genes. This capture is the hidden mass behind the cross-phase 'offline only, acceptable' hand-wave, and if quantization drift is real it re-pays per model ARTIFACT (fp16 parent ≠ Q4 child ≠ next finetune), turning 'once per profile' into a treadmill. The roadmap even concedes GGUF-only models 'cannot get a J-space profile without a llama.cpp fork.'
points.evidence: Grep output_hidden_states → only the roadmap doc, 0 code files. hardware.py:332-352 _BATCH_TABLE (encoder models only, VRAM caps at 24GB tier). roadmap L283-289 + L307-309 (per-artifact profiles / llama.cpp-fork caveat). SIKE corpus sizes 46,517 / 80,363 genes per docs/benchmarks/2026-07-05-sike-bedsweep-issue-resolutions.md §1.
points.targets: Phases 0/2/3/5 prerequisite capture; decision-point #5

points.title: Bet B's success actively strands Phases 2-5 by re-baselining Phase 0 upward
points.claim: Concession: Phase 1 (embedding-space splats + Ledoit-Wolf/Mahalanobis density know/go) is the genuinely strong bet — it needs no decoder internals, has a published blueprint, and upgrades live know/miss work; the cosine→Mahalanobis whitening is a real win available now. But that is precisely the CON case against the expensive J-space phases: Phase 0's pre-registered bar is 'beat BGE-M3 COSINE by ≥5 Recall@10.' Once Phase 1 ships whitened/Mahalanobis BGE-M3, the correct baseline becomes 'beat WHITENED BGE-M3,' which absorbs the same residual-stream anisotropy the J-space probe is chasing — cheaply, in embedding space, with no capture. The ≥5-point margin is set against a baseline the roadmap itself is about to obsolete. Re-baseline Phase 0 against the Phase-1 whitening and the headroom for Phases 2-5 most likely evaporates — especially on the multi-token identifier needles (claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, PostgreSQL 16) where the review already flags the J-lens vocabulary ceiling as weakest.
points.evidence: roadmap L85-87 (Phase 0 bar vs 'BGE-M3 cosine'). review L47 (J-lens vocabulary-bounded, weak on multi-token identifiers — our actual gaps), L50-52 (Mahalanobis blueprint, anisotropy, whitening now), L77 (lead with bet B). scoring/know_calibration.py 5-feature logistic is the incumbent Phase 1 upgrades.
points.targets: Phase 0 success bar; bet B vs bet A sequencing; decision-point #2

The one bench-validity doc that IS in the tree (2026-07-05) states the body/content-has-answer metric has been a 'dead metric... never produced signal' across every run.
