specialty: ML systems / serving (offline hidden-state capture, quantization drift, soft-prefix serving)
verdict_one_line: Phases 0-4 are honestly de-risked, but Phase 5 is mis-sized as a "backend plugin" when it is really a proxy-to-server inversion, and the whole bet's true central risk is that the default deployment (Ollama/GGUF, helix.toml:168) exposes no hidden states and much of its catalog has no fp16 parent to calibrate against.

points.title: Phase 2's latency worry is inverted — the expensive forward pass is already paid
points.claim: Phase 2 frames the hard problem as 'the query arrives on a stack (Ollama) that exposes no hidden states' and sets a '<10ms CPU projection head' budget, but the default stack ALREADY runs a full BGE-M3 query forward pass at request time. A projection head is negligible marginal cost on top of an encode that already happens. The real Phase 2 question is not latency or query-time internals; it is whether the roadmap's own fallback (b) — a LINEAR probe from the in-process BGE-M3 vector to J-coords — hits tau>=0.8. If it does, Phases 2+3 collapse to 'store one d x r matrix in spaces.basis and whiten,' with no new encoder. Neutral read: make the linear-probe-on-BGE-M3 the PRIMARY hypothesis, not the fallback, because the input vector is free and already computed in-process.
points.evidence: context_manager.py:2342 calls query_docs_ann with query_text at request time gated on _dense_embedding_enabled; helix.toml:330 dense_embedding_enabled=true (default); BGEM3Codec defaults device='cpu', use_fp16=False (bgem3_codec.py:79,112). Roadmap Phase 2 fallback (b) and Open Q#2 already name the linear-probe outcome.
points.targets: Phase 2 (query-side J-projection); decision-point Open Q#2

points.title: Phase 5 'new backend in backends/' understates the lift: it is a proxy-to-server inversion
points.claim: Phase 5's schema line ('New backend in backends/ implementing an embedding-injection client; [ribosome] backend = hf_softprefix') conflates two different model seams. The [ribosome] backend is the CPU COMPRESSION model (compressor/deberta). The decoder Helix shapes context FOR is the UPSTREAM, reached purely by HTTP proxy — Helix never loads decoder weights. Soft-prefix injection requires Helix to STOP proxying and OWN the decoder in-process (load HF/vLLM weights, manage its KV cache). That is an architectural inversion from 'transparent proxy' to 'serving stack,' not a backend plugin. Effort XL is right, but the schema-change description hides that nothing in the codebase serves a decoder today.
points.evidence: helix.toml:168 upstream='http://localhost:11434' (Ollama), reached via server/routes_context.py proxy; grep for vllm|prompt_embeds|output_hidden_states|inputs_embeds|AutoModelForCausalLM across all *.py returns zero matches; backends/ contains only CPU encoders/compressors (compressor, deberta, nli, splade, bgem3, sema).
points.targets: Phase 5 (activation-level injection)

points.title: vLLM prompt_embeds and Automatic Prefix Caching are in tension — the KV-reuse story may not hold
points.claim: The review cites 'vLLM prompt_embeds + Automatic Prefix Caching' as a Phase 5 enabler, but from a serving standpoint they fight: APC keys cached KV blocks on a hash of the TOKEN-ID prefix, while prompt_embeds bypasses tokenization and feeds raw vectors with no stable token-id to hash, so injected splat prefixes generally will NOT be APC-dedup'd — every request recomputes the prefix KV. That inverts the cost model: a shared TEXT preamble IS prefix-cacheable, so soft-prefix genes can be MORE expensive to serve than the text they replace. Phase 5's 'count prefix vectors at their KV cost' is the right instinct but must also count the LOST prefix-cache reuse the text path gets for free.
points.evidence: Roadmap Phase 5 validation ('matched effective budget (count prefix vectors at their KV cost)') and review line 37 (prompt_embeds + APC). vLLM APC hashes token-id blocks; prompt_embeds has no token ids — verify against the pinned vLLM version before this is load-bearing.
points.targets: Phase 5 (soft-prefix serving cost model)

points.title: Phase 5 inherits the quantization-drift risk in its worst form — no downstream safety net
points.claim: Open Q#1 (quant drift) is scoped to Phase 0/3 OFFLINE calibration, with mitigation 'spaces row absent -> jspace tier silently off.' But a Phase 5 soft prefix is rendered against fp16 hidden-state geometry and then injected at INFERENCE into whatever the server actually runs. Unlike retrieval (Phase 3, which has re-ranking downstream), an off-manifold prefix directly steers generation with no correction stage. And you cannot measure the drift on the DEFAULT deployed artifact at all, because Ollama serves quantized GGUF with no exposed hidden states. So Phase 5 is only coherent on a non-default fp16 serving stack that Helix must own anyway (see prior point) — meaning Phase 5 and the default Ollama deployment are mutually exclusive, not 'an opt-in profile on the same box.' The roadmap should state plainly that entering Phase 5 = abandoning the proxy thesis for that deployment.
points.evidence: helix.toml:168 default upstream Ollama (GGUF); roadmap Open Q#1 confines drift to offline calibration; Phase 5 'Soft prompts are model- and even finetune-specific' already concedes fragility but not the no-measurement-on-deployed-artifact consequence.
points.targets: Phase 5; decision-point Open Q#1 (quant drift), review decision #3

points.title: Phase 0 measurement under-specifies the deployed target: 'a llama.cpp debug build' is not the Ollama artifact
points.claim: Open Q#1's plan ('compare fp16 vs quantized hidden states on one model via a llama.cpp debug build') measures the wrong artifact. The default decoder is served by OLLAMA, which wraps llama.cpp with its own quant defaults (typically Q4_K_M per tag), prompt templating, and rope/KV settings. To get drift for the DEPLOYED artifact you must reproduce Ollama's exact GGUF quant + build flags, not a generic debug build. Decision-point #5 ('which single fp16 model — must have a real fp16 parent') is the actual gate: a large share of Ollama's catalog is community GGUF-only with no fp16 parent, so the calibratable set is a strict SUBSET of what users run. Pin the exact Ollama tag/quant, not just 'a Qwen or Llama variant,' before Phase 0, or the knee/drift numbers will not transfer to production.
points.evidence: helix.toml:168 upstream=Ollama; helix.toml:35 model='gemma4:e2b' (an Ollama tag); roadmap cross-phase note already concedes 'GGUF-quantized with no fp16 counterpart cannot get a J-space profile'; review decision-point #5.
points.targets: Phase 0 (measurement design); decision-points Open Q#1 and review #5

points.title: The splice floor is a query-blind positional cut — output-shape work is built on sand until it is fixed
points.claim: Grounding the review's splice-truncation flag precisely: the default splice target is a uniform, query-agnostic 1000-char cap, and when Headroom is unavailable the compressor degrades to a raw HEAD slice content[:target_chars].strip(). The rank-proportional foveated cap exists but is OFF by default (foveated_caps None -> target=1000). For Phases 4/5 this is load-bearing: 'injection-shape targeting' assumes control over what text reaches the decoder, yet the default last stage truncates by POSITION, not by where the answer or J-coverage lives — so any J-coverage-optimal selection upstream can still be silently head-cut here. This is the cheap, bet-independent prerequisite (query-aware / budget-proportional cap, or emit complement), not a nicety, and it should land before any coverage-shaped assembly.
points.evidence: context_manager.py:1706 'target = 1000' (uniform branch); :1703-1704 foveated per-rank cap only when foveated_caps is not None; headroom_bridge.py:227 fallback 'return content[:target_chars].strip()'. Review lines 71-72 flag this at the floor; the off-by-default foveated nuance is added here.
points.targets: Phase 4 (coverage-shaped assembly), prerequisite; also gates Phase 5 realism

verdict_one_line: Ship bet B first and stop calling it a hedge: the density know/go is the only phase that compounds Helix's actual moat (the legible know/miss contract), and both it and the J-space tier plug into extension seams the codebase already built for exactly this — the bet's activation energy is far lower than the roadmap prices it.

points.title: Phase 4 isn't greenfield — the foveated splice path is coverage-shaping v0, already shipped and flag-gated
points.claim: The roadmap treats Phase 4 (coverage-shaped assembly) as the risky payoff and the review treats the splice fix as a chore to clear 'before' it — but Helix already ships a per-document non-uniform splice-cap vector (foveated_caps) that the assembler consumes today. Phase 4 is a swap of the function producing that cap vector (relevance-proportional → J-coverage), not a new pipeline stage. That is the plan's strongest 'earns its complexity' argument: the seam exists, it's just default-off.
points.evidence: context_manager.py:1703-1706 overrides the uniform target=1000 with foveated_caps[idx]*foveated_base per document; config.py:159 foveated_enabled=False, :162 foveated_alpha; assembler already accepts respect_caller_order/foveated provenance (context_manager.py:1734-1746).
points.targets: Bet A Phase 4 (payoff phase); rebuts the 'build splice fix first, J-space on top' framing

points.title: Both integration seams (know-logistic, Fuser) were built for additive extension — bet B/Phase 3 cannot regress the default path
points.claim: The biggest product-risk objection ('it complicates the hot path') is empirically false. The know logistic is a config-driven feature vector where adding density is one N_FEATURES bump + one beta + one term; the Fuser treats a zero-weight tier as a silent no-op, so a jspace tier is strictly additive, ships disabled, and cannot move the default ranking. That is why leading with bet B is not just best-supported — it is structurally free to try.
points.evidence: know_calibration.py:84 N_FEATURES=5, :64 DEFAULT_BETAS is a 6-tuple, :277-278 the freshness term is the exact one-line pattern a 6th density feature copies; calibrate_know_confidence.py is the wired recalibration path. fusion.py:104 `if weight == 0.0: return` matches the roadmap's Phase 3 add_tier('jspace', ranked, weight=cfg...) claim verbatim.
points.targets: Bet B Phase 1 (6th logistic feature) + Bet A Phase 3 (jspace Fuser tier); Decision-point #2 (lead with bet B)

points.title: Density know/go is the ONLY phase that compounds Helix's moat — so kill Phase 5 now, don't merely 'gate' it
points.claim: Helix's defensibility vs vanilla RAG is the legible, calibrated, agent-auditable know/miss contract. Bet B strengthens it — density lets the contract express the failure the scalar logistic cannot ('query landed between two gene modes → abstain with a reason'), directly agent-facing trust calibration. Phase 5 (soft prefix) forfeits exactly this: an agent cannot audit or re-derive an un-decodable prefix. Given 'AI agents are the first-class user,' an illegible-context mode is a category error; strike Phase 5 from the roadmap rather than keep an escape hatch that invites drift toward the one direction contradicting the product thesis.
points.evidence: KnowBlock already carries optional log_density/mahalanobis_top1/space_id fields (roadmap Phase 1); contract emitted from context_packet.py / the /context/packet route (CLAUDE.md HTTP surface). Review §Phase-5 concedes soft prompts occupy a 'non-natural-language, un-decodable manifold' (prompt waywardness).

points.title: The splice-truncation 'discovery' was already documented (2026-07-05) and is a strict Phase-4 prerequisite

Sequencing consequence: Phase 4's secondary measurement ('does jcoverage move the decoder's residual closer to target?') is uninterpretable while the final splice stage still discards the answer via a query-agnostic cut. Fix splice first is correct — and cheaper than implied, because foveated caps + the un-emitted Headroom complement already sit in the tree.

But the committed harness is more mature than the roadmap's 'Helix has no gold retrieval set' premise: a multi-valid-gold needle scorer, a 50-needle set, and a gold-set builder all exist. Helix already shipped the anti-poisoning half (HELIX_DISABLE_LEARN, CWoLa/provenance/claims) and the SIKE echo-contamination harness that exposed the failure. Pairing Phase-1 density know/go with the existing provenance flag yields a story neither vanilla RAG nor a pure embedding store can tell — 'calibrated confidence AND tamper-evidence' — testable on a poisoning set that already exists, at the cost of one logistic feature. That combination, not J-space output-shaping, is the sharpest near-term reason an agent would pick Helix.

points.title: Phase 0's harness is greenfield, not half-built, and its grounding numbers aren't in the tree
points.claim: The review's 'we are further along' case for Phase 0 rests on artifacts that don't exist in the repo, so the go-decision is being made on un-reproducible evidence. Kill-switch: if the eval harness must be built from scratch AND the prior RRF/additive numbers can't be reproduced, Phase 0 is a full research build with no committed baseline to beat, not an S-M probe.

The two docs the review grounds itself on (docs/benchmarks/2026-07-06-sike-run2-fts-depth-fusion.md, 2026-07-06-faithfulness-experiment-runbook.md) are not in docs/ — a grep for 'sike-run2|faithfulness-experiment|content_has_answer|fts-depth-fusion' across docs/ matches ONLY the review file itself, so the 'RRF 0.72 vs additive 0.58' number is prose-only.

points.title: The char-1000 splice cut isn't just 'fix first' — it makes Phase 4's validation uninterpretable
points.claim: Phase 4 pre-registers a >=5% end-to-end EM gain from J-space coverage-shaped selection, but the stage AFTER selection throws the answer away for reasons unrelated to selection, so you cannot attribute any QA delta to coverage-shaping until the splice floor is fixed. The cheap fix is a confound-remover, not merely a cheaper win — it is a hard prerequisite for Phase 4 even being measurable.

The primary splice loop at context_manager.py:1707 compresses g.content and never emits g.complement (the 'dense enough to reconstruct the gist' paragraph, compressor.py:390); complement is only used in the fingerprint/packet path (context_packet.py:239-241) and a secondary branch (:2649), never the main window.
points.targets: Phase 4; 'where our empirical work plugs in' (splice-truncation)

points.title: Phase 5 is architecturally incompatible with the know/miss contract — strike it, don't gate it
points.claim: A positive Phase 5 result would force Helix to ship a mode that breaks its own product thesis. Soft-prefix renders context as un-decodable residual vectors: there is no text to hang a legibility header on, no gene_id for a downstream agent to cite, no know{found,confidence,gene_id_match}/miss{reason} expressible over a KV-cache tensor. This is not a 'clean cap'; entering Phase 5 means maintaining two mutually-exclusive product models (legible-text vs illegible-vector) permanently. Kill-switch fires at roadmap-acceptance, not after Phase 4.
points.evidence: CLAUDE.md defines the know/miss contract on /context/packet and per-document legibility headers as the pipeline's identity; MEMORY.md records 'AI is the first-class user' (agents consume the attributable contract). The review itself flags Phase 5 as a legibility/prompt-waywardness conflict (review lines 59, 66) but still keeps it as a 'research escape hatch'.
points.targets: Phase 5; review decision point #3

points.title: The complexity/value ratio is inverted — the cheap wins live outside the phases, the surface-area cost concentrates inside them
points.claim: The two confirmed, live, bet-independent wins (cosine->Mahalanobis whitening of BGE-M3 for #239, and the splice fix) need zero new tables and zero new config sections. The roadmap's phases are where all the durable surface area lands, and every knob shipping 'off by default' is the tell: capability the product can't yet trust as default is capability it doesn't yet need.
points.evidence: CLAUDE.md already enumerates ~20 [config] sections and a 15-subpackage layout. The roadmap adds 2 SQLite tables (splats, spaces) + fragment-level splat rows, 2 config sections ([splat], [jspace]) + [retrieval] jspace_weight, an ONNX projection-head artifact + training/export path, a new Fuser tier (fusion.py:76 add_tier is the real hook), a LOD path in encoding/, and a soft-prefix backend — plus Open Q#1's quantization drift turning per-model 'spaces' rows into a per-artifact treadmill.
points.targets: Phases 1/3; 'does this bet earn its complexity'

points.title: Bet A is weakest exactly where Helix's only in-house eval measures — identifier recall
points.claim: The J-lens is vocabulary-bounded (can't represent multi-token/non-contiguous spans), so bet A pays most for conceptual retrieval and least for identifier lookup — but Helix's entire proof harness scores multi-token identifiers. So Phase 0-4 either validates on a benchmark it's structurally mismatched to (jspace_weight tunes toward 0, which is Phase 3's own kill condition) or someone must first build a conceptual-retrieval gold set the review admits doesn't exist. The payoff regime and the proof harness don't intersect.
points.evidence: Review lines 47/64: J-lens vocabulary ceiling, needles are multi-token (claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, PostgreSQL 16, 51/52). benchmarks/bench_needle.py is the scorer; scripts/bench_chain/s2_sike_bedsweep_run.py imports bench_needle and scores retrieval via find_needle/check_gold_delivery with 'word-boundary accept-matching' on identifier needles (its own docstring, lines 6-33). Phase 3 kill condition is 'tier weight tunes to ~0' (roadmap TL;DR).
points.targets: Phases 0/3/4; review decision point #5 (eval set)

points.title: Bet B's density know/go re-opens the poisoning hole Helix just shipped a fix for
points.claim: Concede bet B is the best-supported strand and the whitening win is real — but 'density know/go' as a confidence upgrade to #239 is not safe standalone. Mahalanobis anomaly measures adherence to the retrieved context, so a faithful generation of self-consistent poisoned context scores LOW anomaly and passes — raising confidence exactly when the store is contaminated. Gating it behind CWoLa/provenance/claims is real extra integration the S-M Phase 1 estimate doesn't budget.

Git log shows the just-merged mitigation: '39685b7 feat(server): HELIX_DISABLE_LEARN gate — read-only / ephemeral serving'. Know path is a 4-feature logistic extensible to 5-6 (know_calibration.py:17 betas 5-tuple, :277 the >=6 freshness feature); Phase 1 adds density as the 6th beta.
points.targets: Phase 1; review risk-register #1

verdict_one_line: The roadmap is unusually disciplined (falsifiable phases, bounded downside) and its epistemics beat the review's, but the council is being asked to reorder it on the review's evidence — which is not under version control — while the one verified, highest-ROI item (the query-agnostic splice cut) is both un-sequenced and already half-built in the tree.

points.title: The splice floor-fix has no phase — and half of it already ships behind a flag

Do the floor fix (query-aware cap + emit `complement`, generalize the foveated path off BROAD-only) and re-measure `body_has_answer` before funding any J-space phase.

The rank-aware machinery already exists: `_compute_foveated_caps` (context_manager.py:341) + the `foveated_caps` override (1703-1704), gated by `[budget] foveated_enabled` and limited to the BROAD tier. `complement` is never emitted in the generic splice branch (spliced_map gets only `compress_text` output at 1712).

points.title: The review's empirical grounding is not in the repo — do not reorder the roadmap on it yet

The council should treat these numbers as uncommitted until the artifacts land; decision-point 4 ('who verifies UWSH/LatentAudit') must extend to 'commit the SIKE Run-2 docs, the driver, and the metric under its real name before they anchor a reprioritization.'

The metric `content_has_answer` (and the 0.72-vs-0.58 figures) appears ONLY in the review doc; the committed metric is `body_has_answer` (benchmarks/bench_needle.py, revived in commit cb34284). `s3_fts_depth_sweep.py` survives only as `scripts/bench_chain/__pycache__/*.pyc`. The direction is real — the 07-05 doc describes 'Run 2 … fusion A/B' and states 'RRF outperforms additive on the 11 rank-7-42 squeeze-outs' — but the numbers are not reproducible in-tree.
points.targets: Decision-points 2 & 4 / review's grounding section

points.title: Keep the roadmap's epistemics; don't let LatentAudit's 0.942 become a Phase-1 anchor
points.claim: The roadmap's stance ('treat as hypotheses we re-measure on our own models') is more defensible than the review's Deep-Research citation wall, which the review itself flags as suspicious (future-dated arXiv IDs, unverified systems) — yet the review's Phase-1 recommendation is 'mirror the LatentAudit recipe.' Concede the review's real point (anisotropy → Mahalanobis-beats-cosine is textbook-solid, and the cosine→whitening win is bet-A-independent), but keep Phase 1's design driven by our own CWoLa/faithfulness labels, not a possibly-fictional AUROC.
points.evidence: Roadmap lines 8-14 pre-register the 're-measure' stance. Review line 40 self-admits the Deep Research is 'hypothesis-generating scaffolding, not verified truth' with '2605/2606 arXiv IDs … none independently verified'; review line 57 nonetheless says 'Mirror the LatentAudit recipe.' know_calibration.py is a real 5-feature logistic (betas = (-2.0, 2.0, 1.5, 0.7, 1.8), N_FEATURES=5) that the density feature can extend without importing an unverified blueprint.
points.targets: Phase 1 / bet B / decision-point 4

points.title: Legibility is the moat, and the conflict is broader than Phase 5
points.claim: The review flags Phase 5 soft-prefix as illegible, but misses that Phase 1's option to 'replace the logistic with a pure density threshold' is the same conflict in miniature: a Mahalanobis scalar is less auditable than a logistic whose betas map to named signals a downstream agent can inspect. Make 'density is a feature of the legible logistic, never a replacing gate' a standing council constraint — which is also the roadmap's own conservative fallback — rather than a Phase-1 A/B that could quietly retire the contract's explainability.
points.evidence: Roadmap line 122 explicitly contemplates replacing the logistic with a pure density threshold. KnowBlock exposes named auditable fields — exactly-one `know`/`miss`, a `reason` vocabulary, `confidence`, `coordinate_confidence` (schemas.py:281-514). CLAUDE.md's /context/packet contract and MEMORY ('AI is first-class user') make the agent the consumer that reasons over these named signals — a scalar density gate degrades that.
points.targets: Phase 1 / Phase 5 / decision-point 3

points.title: Splat storage grows the store in the same month the team shipped an efficiency-cut design
points.claim: 'Does the bet earn its complexity' is partly a storage-coherence question the roadmap raises but never reconciles. Phase 3 admits ~80 KB/gene per space (d=1024, k=16 fp32); if quantization drift (Open Q#1) forces per-artifact profiles, that cost is multiplicative per (gene × model-artifact) — directly against the repo's current lean direction. The council should require Phase 3 to draw its net-storage story against the efficiency doc, not just name fp16/k=8 mitigations.
points.evidence: docs/design/2026-07-05-efficiency-cost-reduction.md and commit f140e9f drive per-column storage DOWN (fp32/fp16 BLOB packing, '~5×/~10×' on the SEMA/embedding columns). Roadmap Phase 3 (lines 182-186) states the ~80 KB/gene per-space cost and Open Q#1 (lines 303-309) frames quantization drift as 'one-off cost or a treadmill.' The two docs are one day apart and pull opposite directions on storage.
points.targets: Phase 3 / bet B / 'earns its complexity'

points.title: 'Helix is for AI agents' makes Phase 5 user-model-mismatched, not just stack-gated
points.claim: The review argues against Phase 5 on capability grounds (J-lens is vocabulary-bounded, weak on our multi-token identifier needles). The stronger product argument: the first-class consumer is an agent hitting /context/packet that calibrates on the know/miss contract and cannot audit or benefit from residual-stream-optimal soft prefixes — those are illegible to it by construction. So Phase 5 is off-thesis for the stated primary user, while a better-calibrated `know{confidence}` (Phase 1's density feature) is exactly what that user consumes. This is the strongest case for 'cap at the weak form.'
points.evidence: Review line 47 makes the vocabulary-ceiling (capability) argument; review line 59 flags Phase-5 illegibility but scopes it as a stack/values gate. CLAUDE.md documents the /context/packet know/miss contract as the agent-facing trust surface; MEMORY design_ai_first_class_user establishes agents as the primary consumer. Phase 5 (roadmap lines 257-264) requires HF/vLLM and produces un-decodable vectors — unusable by an agent that reasons over legible claims.
points.targets: Phase 5 / bet A strong form / decision-points 2 & 3

verdict_one_line: Proceed and lead with bet B: the roadmap's cheapest wins are already prepaid in the schema and the calibration code, and its refusal to trust the shaky evidence is exactly what bounds the downside — the weakest epistemic joint here is the review, not the plan.

points.title: Phase 3's jspace tier is zero-risk-when-off by construction, not by promise
points.claim: The roadmap rates Phase 3 'mostly ENGINEERING' and says the jspace tier's zero-weight disables it 'consistent with existing knobs' — that is literally true in the code, so the production-integration phase has a safe-by-construction seam already in place. Fuser is a pure data structure with no SQL/KnowledgeStore/telemetry coupling, and add_tier() short-circuits and doesn't even record the tier when weight==0.0. Adding and A/B- backing-out the jspace tier is a genuinely additive, fully reversible change — the plug-in point the review discusses only conceptually is verified to exist and to be free.
points.evidence: helix_context/retrieval/fusion.py:104-108 (weight==0.0 early return, skips _tiers_seen); module docstring lines 24-28 ('pure data structure... no SQL, no KnowledgeStore coupling, no telemetry side effects'); roadmap line 189 matches the real add_tier signature.
points.targets: Phase 3; decision-point 2 (lead with bet B) — de-risks the 'production plumbing' phase

points.title: Phase 1's density-as-6th-feature is a transition the team has already shipped once
points.claim: The strongest reason to 'lead with bet B' is not the review's unverified LatentAudit blueprint (arXiv:2604.05358, flagged unverifiable) — it's that Phase 1's exact proposed integration is a proven in-repo pattern. The know logistic already went 4→5 features once (the Stage-7 freshness_min addition), with the config-owned betas, the fit helper, and the N_FEATURES guard all in place. 'Add density as one more beta in [know], recalibrate via calibrate_know_confidence.py' is therefore a low-variance engineering move, not a research gamble. The review under-sells this by anchoring bet B to an external citation it can't confirm.
points.evidence: helix_context/scoring/know_calibration.py:34-40 (STAGE-7-EXT markers), :57-84 (DEFAULT_BETAS is a 6-tuple, N_FEATURES=5), :277-278 (freshness feature appended), fit_betas_from_features at :354; roadmap lines 118-123.
points.targets: Phase 1 / bet B; decision-point 2

points.title: Bet B's marginal cost is a fit + one backfill, because the embeddings already exist
points.claim: The roadmap's 'prepaid downside' claim for bet B is real at the schema level: μ and Σ can be fit from chunk vectors Helix already persists, so Phase 1 adds no new inference — just two net-new tables and a backfill script following an existing pattern. embedding_dense_v2 is already a BLOB column on genes; splats/spaces are genuinely net-new (absent from the DDL); and cwola_log — the weak-label source the validation section names — already exists. This is the cheapest path to a calibration/LOD upgrade that stands even on a total J-space kill.
points.evidence: helix_context/storage/ddl.py:113 (embedding_dense_v2 BLOB on genes), :634 (cwola_log table); splats/spaces absent from the full table list in ddl.py; roadmap lines 113-117 (new tables) and 117 (backfill mirrors backfill_bgem3_v2.py).
points.targets: Phase 1 / bet B; cross-phase 'what survives a total kill' (roadmap line 292)

points.title: The prepaid splice fix is a verified, bet-independent floor win — but scope it to the truncation path, not the review's 'complement' mechanism
points.claim: The roadmap's coverage-based redundancy elision and the review's splice-fix converge on a cheap win that pays regardless of J-space, and the underlying floor bug is real: the last splice stage compresses with a query-agnostic char cap and, whenever headroom is unavailable or errors, falls back to a raw content[:target_chars] truncation. Fixing this before building coverage-shaped assembly on top is correct. Honest caveat that strengthens the case rather than weakens it: the review's specific mechanism claim — 'the answer often lives only in complement, which is never emitted' — is not grounded where it points; 'complement' does not appear in headroom_bridge.py. So the fix should be pre-registered against the verified truncation path, or the complement claim gets falsified mid-implementation and stalls the prepaid work.
points.evidence: helix_context/context_manager.py:1707 (compress_text, target=1000 or foveated_caps); helix_context/encoding/headroom_bridge.py:227, :255, :258 (content[:target_chars].strip() fallbacks); grep for 'complement' in headroom_bridge.py returns no matches; review lines 72.
points.targets: Phase 4 + 'prepaid splice fix' (review recs 1 & 5)

points.title: The roadmap's epistemic discipline is the real asset — and the review, not the plan, is where the slippage lives
points.claim: The strongest pro-roadmap point is meta: the plan wins precisely because it refuses to downgrade 'unconfirmed' to 'true' and budgets Phase 0's eval harness as 'the single highest-leverage engineering item' to BUILD. The review argues that harness is already 'half-built' and leans on it to compress Phase 0 — but on inspection its own grounding is thinner than the Deep Research it (rightly) distrusts: two of the docs it cites as empirical grounding are not in the repo, the 'just-fixed content_has_answer metric' appears in zero code files (the real scorer is bench_needle.find_needle / body_has_answer), and s3_fts_depth_sweep.py's source is gone (only a .pyc survives). The verified skeleton that DOES exist (bench_needle + the s2 recall pass) is real but partial — which vindicates the roadmap's instinct to construct the harness and pre-register bars rather than assume it. Do not let the review's over-confidence shrink Phase 0's engineering budget.
points.evidence: docs/benchmarks/ contains only 2026-07-05-sike-bedsweep-issue-resolutions.md (the review's cited 2026-07-06-sike-run2-fts-depth-fusion.md and 2026-07-06-faithfulness-experiment-runbook.md are absent); 'content_has_answer' grep hits only the review doc; real scorer at scripts/bench_chain/s2_sike_bedsweep_run.py:352 (body_has_answer via bench_needle.find_needle); s3_fts_depth_sweep.py exists only as __pycache__/*.pyc; roadmap lines 63-67.
points.targets: Phase 0; decision-points 4 (who verifies) & 2 — protects Phase 0's build budget

points.title: The poisoning-pairing the review demands for bet B is already cheap in-repo — so risk #1 is not a blocker
points.claim: The review adds a sharp new risk — the Mahalanobis density gate is blind to a faithful generation of poisoned context (the SIKE echo-contamination failure) — and concludes density must be paired with anti-poisoning. That pairing is cheaply available: HELIX_DISABLE_LEARN is already wired end-to-end (helper, test, and the SIKE bench sweep uses it), and the cwola/provenance/claims scaffolding both docs name exists. So the mitigation is a config-composition problem, not new research; it does not gate shipping bet B. This tightens the case for leading with bet B rather than treating the poisoning risk as a reason to wait.
points.evidence: helix_context/server/helpers.py:35-39 (_learn_disabled / HELIX_DISABLE_LEARN), tests/test_learn_disabled.py, scripts/bench_chain/s2_sike_bed_sweep.ps1 (uses HELIX_DISABLE_LEARN); ddl.py:634 (cwola_log); review risk #1 (lines 63).
points.targets: Bet B risk register (review risk #1); decision-point 2

verdict_one_line: The roadmap's kill-switch discipline is genuinely sound, but the review that upgrades it leans on an empirical spine that isn't on the decision branch and on unverified future-labeled citations — verify those before reordering the plan, and fix the floor splice bug that silently moves the baseline every phase must beat.

points.title: The review's empirical spine is not on the branch under review
points.claim: The review's entire value-add over the roadmap is 'we are further along — the eval harness is half-built' (its recommendation to lead with bet B rests on this). But every artifact it cites for that claim is absent from HEAD. A council member who checks out this branch cannot reproduce the 0.72-vs-0.58 RRF number, cannot run content_has_answer, and cannot read either 'grounding' runbook. The re-scope recommendation (decision-points #1/#2) is grounded on evidence that isn't in the tree it is grounding.
points.evidence: HEAD = docs/jspace-roadmap-council @ de9c92c. `git merge-base --is-ancestor` shows commit 5749247 (content_has_answer, #205/#221) and df9c3ae (faithfulness runbook, #239) are NOT ancestors of HEAD — they live only on feat/fts5-candidate-depth-sweep. `git grep content_has_answer HEAD -- '*.py'` is empty (symbol absent from this branch's Python). `git cat-file -e HEAD:docs/benchmarks/2026-07-06-sike-run2-fts-depth-fusion.md` and the faithfulness-runbook path both return ABSENT. s3_fts_depth_sweep.py exists only as a stray .pyc, not tracked source. The RRF 0.72 / additive 0.58 numbers appear in no file except the review itself.
points.targets: Review credibility as council evidence base; decision-points #1 (three-subspace split) and #2 (lead with bet B); Phase 0 'harness is half-built' claim

points.title: Phase 1 density gate: the named anti-poisoning kill-switch is orthogonal to the threat, and density amplifies it
points.claim: The review lists corpus-poisoning as New Risk #1 but frames it as solvable with 'existing scaffolding (CWoLa, provenance, claims) — Helix already has it.' It doesn't. HELIX_DISABLE_LEARN only gates Stage-6 write-back — it stops the model's own echoed answers from being re-learned (the SIKE echo case), and does nothing about content poisoned at INGEST from source docs. Provenance records where a doc came from, not whether its claim is true. Worse, Mahalanobis density structurally rewards ingest poison: a poisoned cluster is a dense mode, so it scores LOW anomaly = HIGH know-confidence. Pairing 'density + provenance' pairs a poison-amplifier with a source-labeler; neither measures truth, so the pairing does not close the hole the review says it closes.
points.evidence: server/helpers.py:38-51 — `_learn_disabled()` docstring: 'Stage-6 persistence is turned off… persisting the query+response exchange as a new gene'; it gates write-back only. splats/spaces tables do not exist yet (schema grep returns zero). identity/provenance.py tracks source attribution, not veracity. Density = Mahalanobis distance to the retrieved distribution, which a poisoned corpus shifts to include the poison.
points.targets: Phase 1 / bet B (density know/go); review New-Risk #1

points.title: Phase 1's 'near-drop-in blueprint' is an unverifiable citation that the review makes load-bearing while deferring its own verify-gate
points.claim: The review correctly flags the Deep-Research anchors as shaky, then builds on them anyway. Phase 1's concrete recommendation is 'Mirror the LatentAudit recipe' with a specific '0.942 AUROC on Llama-3-8B', and the roadmap's '~16 directions' motivation is sourced to UWSH. Both are cited by future-labeled arXiv IDs the review admits 'none independently verified here', and decision-point #4 asks 'who verifies UWSH + LatentAudit before either becomes load-bearing' — but the Phase 1 recipe already treats LatentAudit as the blueprint. The verify-gate sits downstream of the reliance. This is the exact confirmation-bias failure the review warns against: an 'unconfirmed' claim upgraded to 'cited, therefore actionable' inside the same document.
points.evidence: Review line 35 (LatentAudit, claimed arXiv:2604.05358, 0.942 AUROC), line 38 (UWSH, claimed arXiv:2512.05117, '1,100+ models'), line 40 ('none independently verified here… do not downgrade the re-measure stance to cited-therefore-true'), line 57 ('Mirror the LatentAudit recipe'), decision-point #4. No web access in this environment; the IDs are unverifiable from the repo, so from a council's standpoint they must be treated as zero-evidence, not as a blueprint.
points.targets: Phase 1 recipe; decision-point #4 (verification owner); roadmap 'universal ~16 directions' motivation

points.title: The floor splice bug is the kill-switch that fires before Phase 0 — fixing it moves the baseline bet A must beat
points.claim: The review notes the splice-truncation bug but files it as prep work. The sharper consequence: Phase 0 pre-registers 'J-subspace beats BGE-M3 by >=5 Recall@10, ties are a kill', and Phase 4 measures end-to-end QA — both against TODAY's assembly, which on the default path truncates content to a query-agnostic 1000-char prefix and drops the answer. Fix that mundane bug (query-aware / budget-proportional cap / emit complement) and the retrieval-and-assembly baseline gets stronger, so bet A's >=5-point bar gets harder precisely because the honest control was under-measured. Measuring an exotic bet against a crippled baseline inflates its apparent headroom. Order of operations: fix floor, re-measure, THEN decide if J-space still has a gap to chase.
points.evidence: context_manager.py:1707 — `compress_text(..., target_chars=1000)` in the non-foveated branch (foveated_caps is None → target=1000 by default). headroom_bridge.py:222-227 — when `is_headroom_available()` is False it returns `content[:target_chars].strip()`, a blind prefix cut. The foveated rank-proportional path exists but is off by default, so the truncation is live on the default path. Review line 72 identifies the bug but sequences the whole roadmap on top of it.
points.targets: Phase 0 pre-registered bar; Phase 4 end-to-end measurement; overall sequencing

points.title: Phase 4 fragment-splat storage is priced per-gene but incurred per-fragment (YAGNI / cost under-disclosure)
points.claim: Phase 3 prices the splat store at '~80 KB/gene at d=1024, k=16 fp32' per space and offers fp16/k=8 as the mitigation. Phase 4 then quietly switches the unit: it fits splats on `is_fragment` genes (chunk-level, many per document). The roadmap never prices the fragment multiplier — per-fragment covariance factors can multiply the store several-fold over the per-gene headline, in the plain embedding space alone, before any J-space rows are added. For a plan whose selling point is 'maximum downside is bounded and mostly prepaid', an unbounded per-fragment storage term in the payoff phase is exactly the cost that should be stated up front, not discovered at backfill time.
points.evidence: Roadmap Phase 3 storage line ('~80 KB/gene at d=1024, k=16 fp32'); Phase 4 schema line ('Fragment-level splat rows (is_fragment genes) in `splats`'). is_fragment is a real first-class concept: 28 references across 10 modules including storage/ddl.py, encoding/fragments.py, encoding/tree_chunker.py. No fragment-count-aware storage estimate appears anywhere in the roadmap.
points.targets: Phase 4 (fragment_splats=true); Phase 3 storage-cost accounting; roadmap 'bounded downside' claim

verdict_one_line: The roadmap's own "re-measure, don't trust the citation" stance is the best epistemics in this bundle — and the review plus its sibling lineage doc are quietly eroding it, while the review's own empirical grounding is partly phantom (missing bench docs, a metric that exists nowhere in the tree).

points.title: The review's empirical grounding is partly phantom — verify before it becomes load-bearing
points.claim: The review's 'Where our 2026-07-06 empirical work plugs in' section — the basis for 'the eval harness is half-built, we're further along than the roadmap assumes' — rests on artifacts not in the repo. Both cited benchmark docs (2026-07-06-sike-run2-fts-depth-fusion.md, 2026-07-06-faithfulness-experiment-runbook.md) do not exist. The load-bearing metric `content_has_answer` and the 'RRF 0.72 vs additive 0.58' numbers appear NOWHERE in code or bench scripts — only inside the review doc itself. The `s3_fts_depth_sweep.py` 'server-per-config driver' it calls the skeleton of eval_retrieval.py has no source (only an orphan .pyc in __pycache__). So the review commits a milder version of the sin it pins on the Deep Research: citing sources that can't be verified. Phase 0's central engineering claim ('single highest-leverage item') is NOT prepaid.
points.evidence: Glob docs/benchmarks/2026-07-06-* → none; Grep content_has_answer (case-insensitive, repo-wide) → only docs/design/2026-07-06-jspace-roadmap-review.md; scripts/bench_chain/*.py has 4 files, no s3_fts_depth_sweep.py; git ls-files shows none tracked/untracked
points.targets: Decision-point #2 (lead with bet B) and Phase 0's 'harness half-built' premise

points.title: Asymmetric epistemic standard: the Anthropic paper is exempted from the review's own 'verify before load-bearing' rule
points.claim: The review correctly demands 'treat the Deep Research as hypothesis-generating scaffolding, not verified truth' and 'do not downgrade the re-measure stance to cited-therefore-true' — then does exactly that for the Anthropic Global Workspace paper, tagging it 'verified read' and letting its 'Bottom line' hinge on 'It now exists, is published, and is causal.' But both inputs share the same provenance chain (operator-pasted, unfetchable), and the paper (Feb 2026) is unverifiable from here. The sibling lineage doc drops the hedge entirely — 'core premise was just externally validated,' 'the paper validates the mechanism.' The roadmap author's scrupulous 'treat both as hypotheses we re-measure' is epistemically superior and is being eroded across the council docs.
points.evidence: review lines 12/26/40 (verified read vs Deep-Research caution asymmetry); docs/councils/2026-07-06-roadmap-lineage.md:8-9,25 ('externally validated', 'the paper validates the mechanism'); paper date Feb-2026 is past assistant knowledge cutoff, no local copy or quote-checkable excerpt in repo
points.targets: Review Bottom line; Phase 0 kill-bar; Decision-point #4 (who verifies)

points.title: Phase 1's success metric is coupled to the corpus-poisoning blind spot it's supposed to be independent of
points.claim: The review's new risk #1 (density gate blind to poisoning) understates the coupling: Phase 1 defines success as 'density variant dominates the risk-coverage curve — fewer wrong knows' scored against CWoLa-derived labels harvested from the SAME corpus the system was allowed to learn from. A tightly-clustered poisoned region (the SIKE echo-contamination HELIX_DISABLE_LEARN exists to stop) produces LOW Mahalanobis anomaly = HIGH density AND self-consistent CWoLa labels, so it validates as high-confidence 'know.' The density gate would raise confidence on exactly the contaminated cluster. Concrete gap: the roadmap's Phase 1 validation never says to run under HELIX_DISABLE_LEARN or on a held-out clean corpus — without that, the metric can't distinguish 'well-calibrated' from 'confidently echoing poison.'
points.evidence: roadmap Phase 1 validation lines 127-132; HELIX_DISABLE_LEARN real at helix_context/server/helpers.py:35-40 + tests/test_learn_disabled.py; review risk #1 (lines 63) names the failure but not the metric coupling
points.targets: Phase 1 (bet B) validation design

points.title: Phase 0 — the self-described 'kill-shot' — is defeasible by fallback proliferation and has no multiple-comparisons control
points.claim: Phase 0 pre-registers the BAR (+5 Recall@10) but not the config BUDGET. The sweep is {layers ≥3} x {ranks 4/8/16/32/64} x {pooling: mean/last-token/per-chunk} x {cosine/Mahalanobis} = 100+ configs against a 200-500-pair eval set; with a 5-point bar, something clears +5 by chance. Worse, the within-phase fallback ladder ('try last-token, then per-chunk before concluding') plus the review's ADDED windowed/Delta-PCA fallback are new researcher degrees of freedom that WIDEN the escape hatch — making the kill harder to fire, not easier ('we didn't beat BGE-M3, but we haven't tried windowed pooling yet'). A kill-switch that can always find one more pooling scheme does not kill. Fix: pre-register the full grid + a held-out confirmation split; a win must replicate out-of-sample, not merely appear in the sweep.
points.evidence: roadmap Phase 0 method lines 74-92 (rank/layer sweep + pooling fallback ladder, bar '≥5 points Recall@10 at some (layer,rank)'); review lines 36/56 adds windowed pooling as a further fallback
points.targets: Phase 0 (kill-shot) pre-registration

points.title: The 'future-dated arXiv IDs' heuristic is arithmetically miscalibrated — fetchability is the real axis, and it puts the Anthropic anchor in the same bucket
points.claim: The review flags 'future-dated 2605/2606 arXiv IDs' as suspicious, but relative to the doc's own 2026-07-06 date, 2605/2606 = May/June 2026 are PAST, not future — the heuristic's arithmetic is off. It also flags UWSH (2512.05117 = Dec 2025, the most date-plausible ID) as unverified while accepting the Feb-2026 Anthropic paper as verified. The correct verification axis is independent fetchability, not the YYMM stamp — and by that axis the Anthropic paper is in the SAME unverified bucket as the IDs being flagged. Decision-point #4's verification list should therefore ADD the Anthropic paper, not treat it as the trusted anchor the others are checked against.
points.evidence: review lines 40 (‘future-dated 2605/2606’) and 20/38 (UWSH 2512.05117 flagged unverified) vs 21/26 (Anthropic ‘verified read’); arXiv YYMM convention: 2605=2026-05 < doc date 2026-07
points.targets: Decision-point #4 (verification ownership) and the review's citation-triage

points.title: YAGNI: Phase 1's multi-space `spaces` schema pre-builds for a Phase 3 that Phase 0 is allowed to kill
points.claim: Phase 1 is sold as 'bet B decoupled from bet A — stands on its own on a Phase-0 kill,' yet its schema introduces BOTH a `splats` table with a composite (gene_id, space_id) PK AND a `spaces` table carrying a kind='embedding'|'jspace' enum, basis/center BLOBs, and per-layer rows — of which Phase 1 registers exactly one (embedding/BGE-M3/1024). All the multi-space generality exists only to serve Phase 3, which Phase 0 has a real probability of killing (roadmap says Phases 2-5 die on a Phase-0 negative). The honest decoupled-B table is single-space: splats(gene_id PK, mean, cov_factors, cov_logdiag, ...) with no `spaces` table. Adding space_id later is a cheap migration; the roadmap's own honesty principle ('degrade to current behavior, minimal surface') argues for not shipping J-space's schema future inside a bet that's supposed to survive J-space's death.
points.evidence: roadmap Phase 1 schema lines 112-115 (spaces table + composite PK, 'Phase 1 registers one row... Phase 3 adds J-space rows'); Phase 0 kill criterion lines 51-57/90-93 (kill Phases 2-5)
points.targets: Phase 1 (bet B) schema decision
